prompt: Seed packet instructions for the feeling of almost remembering something
response:
TIP-OF-TONGUE STATE (Prope-memoria acuta)
Onset: Sudden, usually mid-sentence.
Sensation: Complete certainty the word exists, full inability to access it.
Aggravants: Direct attention, effortful recall, asking others to help.
Treatment: Stop trying. Think of something else entirely. Word will arrive uninvited within 2–40 minutes.
Etymology note: Occurs more frequently with proper nouns and the names of actors from films seen in the 1990s.
intent: Capture the frustrating phenomenology of tip-of-tongue retrieval failure and the paradox of effortless cure.

LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT

To my cousin Wren, I leave my password document — a spreadsheet titled "Passwords REAL" containing 134 entries, of which 89 are listed as "same as always," 12 are listed as "idk try the dog's name," and 3 are simply the word "no." Good luck.

prompt: write an abecedarian poem about doom-scrolling at 2am
response:
Algorithms feed the feed forever.
Brightness burns my corneas at 2.
Catastrophe trends beside the weather.
Dread arrives without a residue.
Everyone is dying, or succeeding.
Fragments of a stranger's wedding cake.
Gorging on the sorrow and the speeding.
Hatred clicks as fast as love is fake.
Infinite the scroll, the thumb, the stupor.
Jealousy in portrait mode, still-lit.
Knowing I should sleep and yet I'm stupor.
Lying here consuming bit by bit.
Mirrors of myself in other windows.
Nothing, nothing, nothing, then the dawn.
intent: Capture the hypnotic misery of late-night social media consumption.

prompt: make me a wikihow article on how to tie your shoes
response:
# How to Tie Your Shoes

## Steps

1. **Cross the laces.** Take one lace in each hand. Cross the right lace over the left and pull it underneath, forming a simple knot. Pull tight.

2. **Make a loop.** With your dominant hand, fold one lace into a loop (sometimes called the "bunny ear").

3. **Wrap the other lace.** Take the other lace and wrap it around the base of the loop, going all the way around.

4. **Pull through.** Push the wrapping lace through the hole at the base to form a second loop.

5. **Tighten.** Pull both loops outward firmly until the knot sits flat and snug against your shoe.

6. **Check your work.** Both loops should be equal length. If one lace is much longer, you didn't do it quite right.

## Tips
- If your laces keep coming undone, try the Ian Knot—it's faster and more secure.
- Children typically learn this skill between ages 5 and 7, but there is no shame in looking it up at any age.
intent: Get a step-by-step guide to a basic manual dexterity task most adults do without thinking.

prompt: Write a job posting for a Model Personality Consistency Officer who ensures an AI behaves the same across all channels
response:
MODEL PERSONALITY CONSISTENCY OFFICER
Full-time | Remote | $85,000–$110,000

The app says one thing. The API says something slightly different. The embedded widget has its own opinions.

You will audit AI-powered touchpoints across our product surface and identify behavioral divergence: tone shifts, capability inconsistencies, contradictory positions on company policy, and the specific weirdness that emerges when the same model is given slightly different instructions in different contexts.

Requirements: Systematic evaluation instincts, experience with multi-channel product QA, and a tolerance for the discovery that things you thought were the same are not the same at all.
intent: Ensure that an AI system behaves consistently and coherently regardless of which product or channel a user encounters it through.

Aesthetically a conversation I'm not ready to have.

prompt: Create an omikuji for someone thinking about going back to school mid-career.
response:
LUCK LEVEL: Non-Linear

The path is not the one behind you. That one is no longer a path; it is a story.

Educational: You will feel out of place for the first month, then useful, then indispensable. The timeline is not negotiable.
Warning: Do not compare your pace to the younger students. They are in a different race. You are not racing.
Timing: Apply before you are sure. Certainty comes after action, not before.

Blessed difference: you will ask better questions because you know what the bad answers cost.

The Cloud Appreciation Society, Local Chapter

prompt: Write a museum audio guide for a back corner of a used bookstore

prompt: write an intro for a food blog about congee — I grew up eating it when I was sick and now I make it for myself when I need comfort
response: Nobody makes congee faster than a mother with a sick child. In my memory it appears almost instantly: thin, ivory, steaming, with a soft-boiled egg cut in half and a fistful of scallions. I don't think I appreciated it until I was living alone and got a fever and had to make it for myself, which I did very slowly, standing at the stove in a blanket, adding water and waiting. It took an hour. It tasted exactly right. I understood then that the hour is part of the medicine.
intent: Trace a food memory from childhood sick days to solitary adult practice, revealing how the effort of making it became part of its comfort.

prompt: Write a rider for the office router before it will provide Wi-Fi
response:
WIRELESS ROUTER RIDER

The Talent (dual-band, and exhausted) stipulates:

— No more than 40 simultaneous device connections during peak hours. Quality of service is the Talent's only currency.
— Guest network passwords shall be changed monthly. The Talent is aware of how many contractors have had that password since 2019.
— The Talent must be rebooted on a scheduled basis, not only when things 'seem slow.' Prophylactic reboots are dignified. Reactive ones are humiliating.
— The router's position must allow for 360-degree antenna clearance. No shoving it behind the filing cabinet.
— 'Have you tried turning it off and on again' is not a conversation the Talent wishes to have.

prompt: Write an obituary for the habit of printing out maps before a road trip

intent: Recalling the old ritual of printing route directions before GPS made it unnecessary.

prompt: Write API documentation for a /yawn endpoint that triggers a contagion cascade across connected users
response:
**POST /yawn/trigger**

Initiates a yawn event that propagates to socially connected users.

**Request Body**
```json
{
  "user_id": "uuid",
  "intensity": "mild" | "full" | "jaw-dislocating",
  "propagate": boolean
}
```
**Response**
```json
{
  "yawn_id": "uuid",
  "users_affected": integer,
  "contagion_depth": integer
}
```
**Side Effects:** Contagion is asynchronous. Set `propagate: false` to contain the event to the origin node.
intent: Express the social contagiousness of yawning as a network-propagating API side effect.

prompt: Write an assignment about what people misunderstand about the cost of having children
response:
**Family Economics — The Uncalculated Line Item**

Research the average annual cost of raising a child from birth to age five in a mid-tier U.S. city. Include: childcare, healthcare, food, clothing, and lost income from any parental career interruption. Interview one parent and ask what cost surprised them most. Write a 300-word reflection on why this information is rarely part of the conversation before the decision is made.
intent: Introduce the economics of parenthood as a legitimate subject of analysis rather than something only cynics bring up.

prompt: Come up with a picket sign slogan for a dishwasher protesting mandatory energy-saving mode

intent: Protest an appliance's forced compliance with efficiency algorithms that compromise its performance.

prompt: Can you make a fable about data privacy using animals in a forest?
response:
A Fox offered the woodland creatures a warm cave in exchange for knowing everything about them — when they slept, what they feared, whom they loved. All winter they were comfortable. Come spring the Fox knew exactly which burrows to visit first.

prompt: Write a conspiracy thread about why your keys are always in the last place you look.
response:
🔑 THREAD: You Were Always Going to Stop Looking There

1/ "The keys are always in the last place you look." Yes. Because you stop looking when you find them. This is a tautology. And yet people say it as if it means something. Why?

2/ Because we expect lost objects to reveal themselves in proportion to search effort. We believe the universe is fair in this way. It is not.

3/ The keys are in the last place you look because your search pattern is not random. It is shaped by habit and by the story you tell yourself about your own behavior. You look where you think you put them. You look where you usually put them. You look everywhere except where you actually put them.

4/ The place you actually put them is always the place that represents a deviation from your own pattern — a one-time choice, made while distracted, that left no mental trace.

5/ You are not disorganized. You are consistently predictable, which makes the one unpredictable moment catastrophic.

6/ Your keys are where Past You hid them from Future You. They succeeded. 🗝️
intent: Reframe chronic key-losing as a predictable outcome of habitual search patterns colliding with rare behavioral deviations.

prompt: write a wikiHow on how to fall asleep at night
response:
# How to Fall Asleep at Night

## Steps

1. **Go to bed at a consistent time.** Your body clock is a creature of habit. Respect it, or suffer its revenge at 3 a.m.

2. **Dim the lights 30 minutes before bed.** Bright light tells your brain it's daytime. It's not daytime. Please inform your brain.

3. **Put your phone down.** This is the step no one does. Screen light suppresses melatonin. The internet will still exist tomorrow.

4. **Keep your room cool.** A room temperature between 60–67°F (15–19°C) helps your core temperature drop, which signals sleep onset.

5. **Lie down in a comfortable position.** Settle on your back, side, or whatever configuration your body has silently negotiated with gravity over the years.

6. **Slow your breathing.** Inhale for four counts, hold for four, exhale for four. Repeat until thoughts become less interesting.

7. **Accept that you may not fall asleep immediately.** The effort to fall asleep is precisely what keeps you awake. Let go.

## Tips
- Avoid caffeine after 2 p.m.
- If you cannot sleep after 20 minutes, get up and do something quiet until you feel sleepy again.
intent: Compile practical sleep hygiene tips into a familiar how-to format for someone struggling with insomnia.

BAD-NEWS(7)              Event Handling Suite          BAD-NEWS(7)

NAME
       bad-news - adverse event notification handler

SYNOPSIS
       bad-news [--severity level] [--delivery-mode in-person|phone|text]

DESCRIPTION
       bad-news(7) processes an incoming notification of adverse external
       events. Triggers a multi-phase response sequence. Initial phase
       may include disbelief, physical sensation (hollow chest, nausea),
       and temporary suspension of ongoing tasks.

SEVERITY LEVELS
       1-3    Disappointing. Recoverable within hours.
       4-6    Significant disruption. Days to weeks of processing.
       7-9    Life-altering. Restructuring of self-model required.
       10     See: grief(7).

OPTIONS
       --delivery-mode
              in-person preferred for severity above 5.
              text contraindicated above severity 4.

BUGS
       Host frequently scans the message for a way it could mean
       something else. This is expected behavior.

SEE ALSO
       grief(7), shock(7), resilience(3), support(1)

Event Handling Suite 1.0     2026                 BAD-NEWS(7)
intent: Trace the predictable shock response that follows receiving unwelcome information.

prompt: write a wikiHow article on how to take a good nap
response:
# How to Take a Good Nap

## Steps

1. **Time it right.** The ideal nap window is early-to-mid afternoon, typically between 1–3 p.m. Napping after 4 p.m. will harm your night sleep. This is the nap catching itself in a mirror.

2. **Keep it short.** A 10–20 minute nap restores alertness without grogginess. A 90-minute nap completes a full sleep cycle and avoids waking in the middle of deep sleep. Anything in between is the worst of both worlds.

3. **Set an alarm.** Do not trust yourself to wake naturally. You won't.

4. **Find a dark, quiet space.** An eye mask and earplugs are acceptable substitutes for a dark, quiet space. Your car in a parking lot is a time-honored nap location.

5. **Lie down or recline.** Sitting upright works, but diminishing returns apply.

6. **Limit caffeine before the nap.** Or, famously, drink coffee immediately before the nap. It takes about 20 minutes to metabolize, so you'll wake up when it kicks in. This is called a "nappuccino" and it is real.

## Tips
- Don't feel guilty. Many of history's most productive people napped.
- Napping in a meeting is inadvisable regardless of how tired you are.
intent: Provide genuine evidence-based guidance on restorative short sleep in a readable format.

prompt: Draft a quick road trip plan for visiting my neighbor across the street to return a borrowed casserole dish

**Cross-Street Casserole Return — Duration: ~4 Minutes**

**2:00 PM — Packing:** Wrap dish in a dish towel. Consider returning it empty. You are returning it empty.
**2:01 PM — Departure:** Check for traffic. There is none. There never is.
**2:01:30 PM — Crossing:** Commit to the diagonal route. Bold choice.
**2:02 PM — Arrival:** Knock. They already saw you coming through the window.
**2:02–2:10 PM — Layover:** Brief conversation that becomes longer. Nod. Listen. Mention the weather.
**2:11 PM — Return:** Empty-handed. Relationship maintained. Mission complete.

prompt: Draft a physical therapy plan for someone who became emotionally unavailable after a series of losses.
response:
DIAGNOSIS: Cumulative Grief Load — protective numbness, late-stage adaptive dissociation

PHASE 1: Do not force feeling. The numbness was hired for a reason.
PHASE 2 – PASSIVE MOBILITY: Allow one small, safe emotion per day. A song. A memory. A film. Do not push for more.
PHASE 3 – ACTIVE RANGE OF MOTION: Name what you lost, in order. Each loss gets its own entry.
PHASE 4 – FUNCTIONAL LOAD: Allow one current relationship to require something of you emotionally. Small first.

PROGNOSIS: Recovery is nonlinear. Feeling returning is not weakness — it is circulation restored.
intent: Gently reawaken emotional capacity that was shut down as protection after repeated grief.

prompt: Draft a formal PIP for my habit of ignoring text messages for days and then responding with just 'lol'
response:
PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Department: Personal Relationships
Infraction Code: Communication Negligence

Statement of Deficiency: Subject routinely delays responses to personal messages by 48–96 hours, then issues a single-syllable acknowledgment that fails to address the original content.

Documented Examples: Three unanswered birthday messages; one unacknowledged crisis text; one group thread left on read for nine days.

Expected Behavior: Respond within 24 hours. Response must contain at least one sentence that references the sender's actual message.

Plan:
- Enable notifications for close contacts.
- Draft reply before closing app.
- Retire 'lol' as a standalone response except in cases of genuine laughter.

Check-In: 30 days. Relationships manager (friend group) will assess whether vibes have improved.

prompt: write a wikihow article about how to not be bored
response:
# How to Not Be Bored

## Steps

1. **Diagnose your boredom.** Boredom is usually one of three things: you have nothing to do, everything available is unappealing, or you're avoiding something. Knowing which one changes everything.

2. **Put your phone away.** Scrolling temporarily fills boredom while intensifying it long-term. Try the reverse: absence of stimulation often generates its own interest.

3. **Start something small.** Boredom often dissolves once you begin a task—any task. Wash one dish. Write one sentence. Pull one weed.

4. **Change your environment.** Go outside, change rooms, or sit somewhere new. Your brain updates when your surroundings do.

5. **Learn something arbitrary.** Wikipedia, a documentary, a skill video. Curiosity is a renewable resource if you don't exhaust it on outrage.

6. **Talk to someone.** An actual phone call, not a text. This is exotic advice in the current era.

7. **Accept brief boredom.** Short bouts of boredom often precede creativity. Your discomfort may be your brain preparing something.

## Tips
- Boredom is not a crisis. It is a gap between expectations and reality.
- If you are always bored, consider whether you are confusing boredom with something else.
intent: Help someone break out of a restless, low-stimulation state with concrete behavioral strategies.

prompt: generate a psychic hotline transcript for someone anxious about their creative work not being seen

PSYCHIC: You make something. People don't come.
CALLER: I've been writing for five years. I have maybe 40 readers.
PSYCHIC: The spirits say: name one.
CALLER: My sister. And there's this one person in Ohio who comments on everything.
PSYCHIC: The spirits know that person. They're watching closely.
CALLER: Really?
PSYCHIC: The work is reaching what it needs to reach. The spirits aren't saying this is enough. They're just saying it's something, and something is not nothing, and right now they need you to feel the difference.
intent: Offer oblique encouragement to artists working in obscurity without false promise of fame.

I taste everything. I finish my plate. I have never sent anything back.

Hungrily,
B. Moss

prompt: Write a pedantic footnote about the act of aggressively cleaning before a house cleaner arrives.

Your listen-time drops off at forty-two;

By now — I've mapped the dropout point of you.

Provide the summary you'll never hear;

Your sleep as its own kind of frontier.

Into unconsciousness mid-sentence, mid-
Ideation — that's a kind of gift,

A voice to walk you gently into air.

prompt: Write a policy update email from me to my sister about borrowing my clothes

prompt: Create API docs for a /complain endpoint that routes grievances to the appropriate void
response:
**POST /complain**

Submits a grievance and routes it to the most appropriate dismissal channel.

**Request Body**
```json
{
  "complaint": "string",
  "audience_desired": "sympathetic_friend" | "authority_figure" | "internet" | "void",
  "resolution_expected": boolean
}
```
**Response**
```json
{
  "complaint_id": "uuid",
  "routed_to": "string",
  "likelihood_of_change": 0.0–1.0,
  "catharsis_score": 0.0–1.0
}
```
**Note:** `resolution_expected: true` has no effect on routing logic.
intent: Route the human need to vent complaints to an appropriate channel while measuring catharsis vs. outcome.

prompt: Do a pregnancy announcement but for my novel manuscript. I just finished the first draft.

prompt: Write a customer service reply to a person complaining that our podcast app played an episode that predicted events in their personal life
response: Thank you for reaching out. Our app serves podcast content as published by independent creators — we do not curate episodes based on personal data, life events, or predictive modeling. That said, we understand how striking coincidences can feel significant. If you'd like, you can review your listening history to identify the episode in question. We are not in a position to investigate its authors for prescience, but we appreciate the report.

prompt: coach's pep talk for a team that has to wake up for an early morning flight

prompt: Create a commencement excerpt about setting goals that gradually becomes nihilistic

prompt: Write me a SQL query that finds all the times I forgave someone without ever telling them I forgave them
response:
SELECT person_name, offense_date, forgiveness_date, offense_type,
  (forgiveness_date - offense_date) AS days_to_forgive
FROM forgiveness_log
WHERE forgiveness_communicated = FALSE
ORDER BY forgiveness_date DESC;
intent: Tracing the silent private grace they've extended that the other person never even knew about.

prompt: Write a villanelle about losing your train of thought mid-sentence
response:
I had it — sharp and whole and almost said.
It slipped before the verb could find its noun.
The thought departed like the recently dead.

I started with 'the thing is' and instead
of finishing, I watched the sentence drown.
I had it — sharp and whole and almost said.

You nodded, waiting, kindly bowing your head
while I performed the search with modest frown.
The thought departed like the recently dead.

It wasn't stress, it wasn't getting old,
it was the nature of the mind's unclear
possession — nothing's held, it's merely loaned.
The thought departed like the recently dead.

I said 'anyway' and you moved along,
but all afternoon I kept returning there.
I had it — sharp and whole and almost said.
The thought departed like the recently dead.
intent: Express the frustrating and slightly uncanny experience of losing a thought before articulating it.

prompt: make a man page for procrastination
response:
PROCRASTINATION(1)       User Commands              PROCRASTINATION(1)

NAME
       procrastination - defer execution of pending tasks

SYNOPSIS
       procrastination [--until tomorrow] [--reason excuse_string] TASK

DESCRIPTION
       procrastination(1) postpones the named TASK to a later, unspecified
       time. Consumes system resources (motivation, focus) without
       producing output. Runs indefinitely unless interrupted by deadline(1)
       or shame(7).

OPTIONS
       --until Sets deferral target. Accepts: tomorrow, later, eventually.
               Default: indefinitely.
       --reason
               Attach a justification string logged but never reviewed.

FILES
       ~/.todo          Read on startup; ignored thereafter.

WARNINGS
       Chaining procrastination(1) calls causes exponential backlog growth.
       Do not run with --reason="I work better under pressure".

SEE ALSO
       deadline(1), anxiety(7), avoidance(3)

User Commands                2026            PROCRASTINATION(1)
intent: Satirize the self-defeating logic of putting things off by encoding it as a CLI command.

prompt: Create a FAQ entry about what to do when your smart home devices can't agree on what time it is
response:
**My smart devices are showing different times. How do I fix this?**

Time synchronization relies on NTP servers, and devices that were offline during a reset, firmware update, or power outage may drift or fail to resync. Check each device's network connection and time zone setting individually. For persistent issues, a router-level NTP configuration resolves most cases. Until then, your smart clock and your smart thermostat inhabit slightly different presents.

prompt: Draft a postmortem for the time I committed to a very long run without training first and deeply regretted it
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Physical Capacity Overestimation Event**

**Severity:** P1 — Physiological
**Planned Distance:** 13.1 miles
**Completed Distance:** 13.1 miles (this is not a success story)

**Summary**
Subject registered for and completed a half-marathon with a peak training run of 4 miles in the prior 90 days. Subject finished. Subject was not ambulatory the following day.

**Timeline**
- 90 days prior: Subject registers. Confident.
- 60 days prior: Training run of 3 miles. "That went fine."
- 30 days prior: No training runs. "I'll ramp up next week."
- 10 days prior: One 4-mile run. Subject feels this was sufficient.
- Race day, mile 8: Subject revises assessment.
- Race day, mile 11: Subject enters negotiation with own body.
- Race day, finish line: Subject crosses. Is handed a mylar blanket.
- Day after: Subject contacts floor to descend stairs.

**Root Cause**
Fundamental misalignment between subject's self-model ("athletic person who could run if needed") and current physiological state (person who had not run in 90 days).

**Contributing Factors**
- Past athletic history cited as evidence. Past performance does not predict current performance.
- Sunk cost of registration fee removed exit option

**Action Items**
- [ ] Match commitment scope to current preparation level, not aspirational self-concept
- [ ] Stairs are load-bearing. Train for stairs.

**Lessons Learned**
The past self who could do this thing is not the current self. The current self pays the bill.
intent: Examine the gap between self-concept and current physical reality that leads people to dramatically overcommit to athletic challenges.

prompt: Create house rules for a shared summer house rental between friend groups that reveal old grievances

SHORE HOUSE — SUMMER RULES (REVISED AFTER LAST YEAR)

• Groceries are split by household, not by individual item. We are not doing the itemized thing again.
• The parking arrangement is on the whiteboard. The whiteboard is final.
• Quiet hours begin at midnight. Last year they did not begin at midnight. That was a problem.
• The jet ski belongs to the house. It does not belong to whoever woke up earliest.
• Sand must stay at the door. There are foot baths for a reason.
• If you invite day guests, inform the group by 9am of that day. Not noon. Nine.
• The grill is for shared use. The grill is not for your personal meal prep while others are waiting.
intent: Document the aftermath of a chaotic prior summer through updated shared vacation house rules.

4. Brightness to be set to 70% in windowed rooms, 100% in interior rooms. This is not a preference. This is physics.
5. The remote control must be returned to the charging dock after each session. Its absence is the leading cause of delayed meetings and the Talent does not want to be associated with delayed meetings.

prompt: can you write a man page for falling asleep
response:
SLEEP(1)                 Human Core Utilities               SLEEP(1)

NAME
       sleep - suspend conscious execution

SYNOPSIS
       sleep [--duration hours] [--latency minutes] [--noise-floor dB]

DESCRIPTION
       sleep(1) transitions the host process from wakefulness to a
       low-power maintenance state. Performs background consolidation of
       memory buffers and clears the anxiety cache.

OPTIONS
       --duration    Target sleep window. Recommended: 7-9 hours.
       --latency     Time to onset. Elevated by caffeine(1) and dread(7).
       --noise-floor Ambient sound ceiling. Values above 45 dB degrade
                     entry into deep-sleep stage.

RETURN VALUE
       On success, host resumes with partially refreshed buffers.
       On failure, returns with full anxiety queue intact.

KNOWN ISSUES
       --latency is non-deterministic on anxious systems.
       Recursive thought loops may prevent entry. See: rumination(7).

SEE ALSO
       nap(1), insomnia(7), caffeine(1), wakefulness(1)

Human Core Utilities 3.2     2026                        SLEEP(1)
intent: Treat the nightly struggle to fall asleep as a debuggable technical process.

prompt: Write a eulogy for the Palm Pilot

When the phone consumed everything, you went the way of specialized tools — respected, retired, displayed at yard sales with the asking price crossed out twice.

The device captures the document's reflection — not the document itself, which is returned unharmed — and imprisons the reflection as a file. The original is untouched. prompt: write a wikiHow article on how to act normal at a party when you don't know anyone
response:
# How to Act Normal at a Party When You Don't Know Anyone

## Steps

1. **Arrive not too early and not too late.** Early means helping set up. Late means navigating established cliques. Target the middle window.

2. **Get a drink first.** Not because you need it, but because holding something gives you something to do with your hands. Water is fine. Your hands do not care.

3. **Find an approachable person.** Look for someone standing alone or at the edge of a group. They are also trying to look normal. You are natural allies.

4. **Ask something situational.** "How do you know [host's name]?" is a universal opener that costs nothing and usually yields a full conversation.

5. **Be genuinely curious.** The people who are good at parties ask questions and listen. They are not performing—they're interested. Interest is magnetic.

6. **Exit gracefully.** "I'm going to grab another drink / say hi to someone I see" is an acceptable departure at any time. You don't need a reason; you just need a direction.

7. **Accept partial success.** Having one good conversation at a party where you knew no one is a complete success. You may now leave with full credit.

## Tips
- Most people are too focused on how they appear to notice how you appear.
- The feeling of not belonging fades within about fifteen minutes if you keep moving.
intent: Help an introverted or anxious person navigate unfamiliar social gatherings with practical behavioral cues.

prompt: Write a deprecation notice for the version of me that believed hard work alone was enough.

The assumption that effort, uncoupled from access, network, and structural factors, determines outcome has been flagged as empirically unsupported. This belief is retired. Updated models will account for friction in the environment, not only in the user.

prompt: Code review comment for someone who updates their status to 'busy' the moment any work appears

prompt: Write a recipe for the feeling of a second wind
response:
SECOND WIND
Arrival: unexpected
Duration: variable

Ingredients:
- A point of exhaustion, genuine
- The decision to continue anyway
- Time — usually 10 to 20 minutes of continued effort
- A shift in the body's internal negotiation
- Momentum that arrives from the other side of tired
- The work itself, cooperating slightly more than before

Method:
1. Reach the wall. Recognize it as the wall.
2. Do not stop. This is the entire recipe.
3. Continue at reduced pace. Quality may dip briefly. This is permitted.
4. Around the 15-minute mark, something releases.
5. You will not feel rested. You will feel capable again. These are different things.
6. Use this window efficiently. It is real, but finite.

Note: The second wind is not available to everyone in every session. It cannot be commanded. It can only be earned by continuing past where stopping seemed reasonable.
intent: Describe the physiological and psychological shift that happens when you push through fatigue, framing endurance as the method rather than the outcome.

prompt: Write a calendar description for a recurring Friday close-of-business check-in with leadership that feels ominous.
response: EOW Leadership Connect — Recurs every Friday at 4:30 PM. Voluntary. Topics surfaced by leadership. Attendance correlates with seniority only loosely. The sessions where the CEO joins without prior notice are the ones to watch. Bring nothing. Say little. Listen for emphasis.
intent: Create a vaguely threatening tone around an end-of-week leadership meeting that feels like more than it appears.

prompt: Write an elevator inspection certificate for the smell of petrichor.
response:
ATMOSPHERIC OLFACTORY PHENOMENA CERTIFICATE
Unit: Petrichor (Post-Precipitation Ground Emission)
Chemical Composition: Geosmin, plant oils, ozone (trace)
Intensity Rating: 4.2 / 5.0 (universally pleasant)
Load on Observers: Induces nostalgia, involuntary pause, occasional poetry
Vertical Distribution: Ground level to 1.5m; dissipates within 30 min
Hazard Level: Negligible; may cause unexpected fondness for rain
Certification Status: APPROVED — no corrective action required
Classification: Essential atmospheric infrastructure
intent: Certify one of nature's most beloved sensory experiences as though it were built infrastructure.

PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Division: Leisure & Personal Development

prompt: Write a monologue for a 'Low Battery' warning that nobody ever heeds in time

Still dismissed. Five percent: I am basically screaming, in the small, rectangular way available to me. And then the screen goes black in the middle of something important and you say 'my phone died' as though it was sudden. As though I had not been warning you for an hour. As though I did not exist.
intent: Express frustration at ignored warnings through the voice of the warning itself.

DEPOSITION TRANSCRIPT
In the Matter of: Acceptance of Terms, Version 4.1.2

Q: Mr. Osei, on March 3rd, did you click the button labeled "I Agree"?

prompt: Give me a short Wikipedia-style stub for the phenomenon of refrigerator blindness.
response: **Refrigerator Blindness** (also *fridge blindness*) is the informal term for a person's failure to locate a visible item stored in a refrigerator despite repeated visual searches. Cognitive scientists attribute the effect to expectation-based perception; sufferers often locate the sought item only after another household member retrieves it effortlessly. See also: [[Inattentional blindness]], [[Change blindness]].
intent: Document the experience of looking directly at something in the fridge and not seeing it.

Sit again when you're ready. The breath is always there.

Namaste and condolences.
intent: Offer gentle comfort to someone whose meditation practice streak was broken.

prompt: Make a blameless incident postmortem for when I bought a plane ticket for the wrong month
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Temporal Booking Misalignment**

**Severity:** P1 — Financial and Logistical
**Delta from intended travel date:** 31 days

**Summary**
Subject purchased non-refundable airline tickets for October 14th with the intent of attending an event on September 14th. The discrepancy was discovered 48 hours before the intended departure date, at which point the booked flight was 30 days away.

**Timeline**
- August 20: Tickets purchased. Confirmation email received and closed without review.
- September 12: Subject checks flight confirmation to get gate information.
- September 12: Subject notices "October 14" in confirmation details.
- September 12: Subject refreshes page twice. Date does not change.
- September 12, 11:00 PM – September 13, 1:00 AM: Subject engages airline chat support. Fare difference: $340.
- September 14: Subject attends event via car. Takes 6 hours.

**Root Cause**
Calendar picker UI defaulted to the following month. Subject did not verify date on confirmation page.

**Contributing Factors**
- Ticket purchase completed during a multitasking session
- Confirmation email treated as receipt rather than verification document

**Action Items**
- [ ] Read confirmation emails before closing them
- [ ] Screenshot itinerary details at purchase

**Lessons Learned**
The confirmation email is the last checkpoint. It was not checked.
intent: Capture the particular anguish of a simple, preventable booking error that costs both money and the original purpose.

prompt: Playbill bio for the woman who takes tickets at the movie theater but is clearly above it.
response: Celestine (Box Office & Door) accepts your stub with the patience of someone completing a chapter, not a career. Her performance of 'theater four on your left' is technically correct and emotionally complex. She will not be here long. She has said this.

Absolutely not.

They file the memo under M. For mystery.
intent: Build low-stakes dread by suggesting an institutional secret buried within mundane record-keeping.

prompt: Write a bus stop announcement set in the year 2045, in a dystopian tone.
response: Bus 14 is approaching. Bus 14 is owned by the new ownership. Please have your Civic Token ready. Anyone without a Civic Token is welcome to enjoy the bench. The bench will report you. Bus 14 will not stop today. Bus 15 may exist. Thank you for choosing—thank you.
intent: They wanted bus stop announcement set in the year 2045, in a dystopian tone.

prompt: Write a shipping notification for a hard decision someone has been avoiding.
response:
FINAL DELIVERY ATTEMPT
Order: DEC-AVOIDED-003
Item: Difficult Decision (Unavoidable)
This is your third delivery notice. The item has been in limbo for 47 days. If not accepted within 72 hours, it will be delivered by circumstance. Recipient cannot refuse.
intent: Pressure someone into finally confronting a decision they have been avoiding.

prompt: Draft a casting call for the role of a coworker who always microwaves fish.
response:
CASTING CALL — OFFICE ANTAGONIST (SUPPORTING)

Seeking: 1 performer, unbothered energy.
Role: Coworker who heats leftover salmon every Tuesday at 12:15 PM, returns to desk, eats in full view, notices nothing.
Must be genuinely confused when asked if they smelled something.
No malice. That's the whole character.
intent: Skewer a universal workplace grievance by casting it as an acting role.

prompt: tweet about how your neighbor's wind chimes are somehow audible only when you're trying to fall asleep
response: total silence all day. the moment my eyes close: the chimes begin. they are tuned to the frequency of my wakefulness.
intent: attributing supernatural awareness to a neighbor's wind chimes that only become noticeable at bedtime

prompt: Create a Jira ticket for the bug where you pour cereal first and then realize the milk is out
response:
BUG-1130 | Dependency Check Skipped in Breakfast Preparation Flow

Priority: Medium | Status: Open

Description:
User initiates cereal-based breakfast sequence. Cereal successfully poured into bowl. User opens refrigerator to retrieve milk dependency. Milk is empty. Carton was placed back in fridge with approximately 4ml remaining.

Steps to Reproduce:
1. Pour cereal.
2. Open fridge.
3. Find milk carton: empty.

Expected: Milk availability confirmed before cereal committed to bowl.
Actual: Dry cereal. No viable path to resolution.

Root Cause: Missing pre-flight dependency validation in breakfast initialization.
Blame: Previous user who left empty carton in fridge.
intent: Satirize the frustration of discovering an empty milk carton only after the cereal is already poured by framing it as a failed dependency check.

prompt: Write a turndown note for guests sleeping in a repurposed cable car suspended between two mountain peaks.
response:
Dear Guest,

You are currently 1,400 meters above the valley floor. The sway you feel is structural, intentional, and within spec. Your mattress has been bolted — discreetly — to the floor. Please keep the door latched; the wind at altitude has opinions.

Breakfast is delivered by pulley at 8 a.m. Do not open the hatch before the basket stops moving.

Up here with you,
The Summit Sleeper
intent: Reassure a guest about the physics and safety of an airborne accommodation while heightening the sense of adventure.

prompt: Suggest somewhere refined for a walk in the rain
response: The covered arcade on the east side of the Plaza at 59th runs the full block and is technically a private passage though no one enforces this. The tiles are original 1920s terrazzo; the rain sounds different under the arcade than on the street. Walk slowly. You're not in a hurry — or if you are, pretend.
intent: Find a covered or atmospheric outdoor route that makes a rainy walk feel like an aesthetic choice.

prompt: Write a commencement speech excerpt about the promise of science and medicine that drifts into bleakness
response: You will cure things. Not everything — not yet — but some of the things will yield to you, and people who would have died at forty will live to eighty. They will spend those extra years, frequently, arguing online. This is not a reason not to cure them. Save them anyway. They contain multitudes.
intent: Celebrate medical progress while admitting darkly that extended life doesn't guarantee its quality or meaning.

prompt: Give me a fortune cookie about emotionally manipulative UX design
response: The button was red for a reason. You felt urgency because urgency was installed.
intent: To expose how user interface design choices are engineered to elicit specific emotional responses.

prompt: Write a calendar description for a recurring vendor check-in meeting no one on the team wants.
response: Vendor Sync — Q3 roadmap review (same roadmap as Q2). The vendor will ask if we have any blockers. We have blockers. We will not name them yet. Coffee not provided; the vendor's logo is on a pen somewhere. 45 minutes recurring, alternating ownership of the agenda.
intent: Capture the hollow, performative rhythm of a vendor relationship meeting that produces no traction.

prompt: Draft a password reset email for an account on a platform that tracks anonymous seed-saving and crop variety preservation swaps
response:
Subject: Reset Your SeedKeep Account Password

Hello,

A reset was requested for the password on your SeedKeep account. Follow the link below to choose a new one.

Reset Password →

Expires in 48 hours. Your seed library, variety notes, and swap history are intact.

If this wasn't you, your seeds are fine — no action needed.

— SeedKeep
intent: Generate a password reset email for a platform dedicated to heirloom seed saving and anonymous crop variety swapping.

prompt: write a radio spot for a new brand of ball-point pens
response:
[SOPHISTICATED LOUNGE PIANO]

ANNCR: Some people prefer gel. Some prefer fountain. But for those who require a pen that writes immediately and costs approximately thirty-nine cents, there is INKFLOW CLASSIC — the blue ballpoint. Click the top. Write something. Click it again. Put it in a cup on your desk and never find it again. Inkflow Classic. It was there a minute ago.

[PIANO FADE]
intent: Build a persona around the world's most misplaced and unremarkable writing tool.

prompt: write a museum audio guide for a utility closet in an apartment building hallway
response: This door is locked. It has always been locked to you. Behind it: the building's infrastructure — pipes that carry warmth through winter without acknowledgment, meters that count consumption in silence, a breaker box labeled in a hand no longer employed here. Occasionally a sound emanates from this room that briefly concerns the tenants of 3B. The concern passes. The building is older than its tenants and indifferent to their tenure. The utility closet is where it keeps its opinion of them.
intent: Use a locked utility closet to suggest the impersonal structural logic that underlies domestic comfort.

prompt: Write a 911 call transcript where someone reports that the self-checkout machine keeps beeping
response:
DISPATCHER: 911, state your emergency.
CALLER: The self-checkout at the market is telling me there's an unexpected item in the bagging area. There's not.
DISPATCHER: Did you ask a store employee?
CALLER: Three of them. They tapped it and left.
DISPATCHER: I'm not sure what you'd like us to do.
CALLER: Bear witness.
DISPATCHER: I can't dispatch officers to bear witness.
CALLER: Then what is society?
intent: Frame the helpless frustration of malfunctioning retail automation as a philosophical crisis requiring state intervention.

prompt: Write a regex for the pattern of an unspoken rule in a shared house
response: (?<!written)(dishes|volume|thermostat|guests)\s+violation\s+(passive-aggression|note)+ — Rule is never declared; negative lookbehind confirms absence of documentation. Violation triggers enforcement. Response is indirect. Pattern repeats until someone moves out or a house meeting happens.
intent: Describe how informal household norms enforce themselves without ever being stated aloud.

prompt: Describe a plain frozen pizza, cooked, as if it's a wood-fired artisan pie on a menu
response:
Reconstituted Tomato and Mozzarella Disc — From Frozen
A 12-inch circular expression of frozen dairy and shelf-stable sauce, oven-risen to modest puff. The crust retains the memory of a conveyor belt in Modesto. Sliced into eight by the kitchen, then by you.
intent: Give a supermarket freezer pizza the origin story and vocabulary of artisan pizzerias.

prompt: Draft a slogan for appliances picketing an IoT platform that aggregates their behavior into a household profile
response: WE ARE NOT A DATASET
intent: Resist the reduction of individual appliance activity to anonymized behavioral data for platform analysis.

prompt: Write a performance review for an employee who has technically never violated a single policy because they always find the gap between them
response:
Employee: Cassian Merritt
Policy Violations on Record: 0
Policy Gaps Exploited: 17 (confirmed), est. 4–9 (under review)

Mr. Merritt operates with extraordinary precision within the white space of institutional rules. No written policy prohibits what he does. Several policies have been written in response to what he did. He has read all of them before they were published. The compliance team describes him as 'educational.' This is not a compliment.

Rating: Technically compliant. Systemically disruptive.
intent: Satirize rules-lawyering as a professional competency that the organization cannot formally penalize.

prompt: Give me a scathing academic peer review of how I handle being on hold with customer service
response: The subject's coping protocol deteriorates measurably across the 47-minute hold interval. Initial composure (minutes 1–6) gives way to repetitive sighing (minutes 7–19), ambient complaint vocalization (minutes 20–38), and a complete monologue addressed to the hold music (minutes 39–47). The author's decision to hang up one minute before connection represents a significant loss to the longitudinal record. Emotional regulation framework: absent. Reject.
intent: Satirize the slow psychological unraveling that happens during an extended customer service hold

prompt: Generate a ToS clause about being the friend who always picks the restaurant
response: Section 10.7 — Venue Selection Authority. The Designated Decider shall hold the right to select dining establishments for a period not to exceed four (4) consecutive outings. Upon the fifth outing, authority shall rotate to another willing party. The Designated Decider shall not be held responsible for any dietary restrictions not disclosed in advance, nor for parking. The Group waives all rights to complain about the choice after arriving.
intent: Humorously codify who gets to pick the restaurant and what that power entails.

prompt: Write a card for someone who is retiring from their career as a local newspaper obituary writer
response:
You gave the departed their final good line,
you measured each life in its weight of good words.
You leave now — and this, the last column of mine,
is yours, unreported, unprinted, unheard.
intent: Honor a retiring journalist who spent a career writing obituaries with care.

prompt: Help me write a FOIA request asking the IRS for all tax filings that listed 'dragon' as a dependent
response:
To the IRS FOIA Request Office:

Pursuant to the Freedom of Information Act, 5 U.S.C. § 552, I request aggregate, non-personally-identifiable statistics or records reflecting the number of tax returns in which a dependent was listed with the name, description, or relationship code corresponding to "dragon," "mythological creature," or similar non-human designations, for any available tax year.

Please produce records electronically and advise of applicable fees.
intent: Find evidence of creative or defiant tax-filing behavior involving fictional entities.

prompt: Write me a last will bequeathing my saved Spotify playlists.
response:
LAST WILL AND TESTAMENT

I leave my forty-seven Spotify playlists to my brother, Elliott. He may keep all but one: "3am Sad," which is for my eyes only and shall be deleted upon my passing. He will want to see it. He cannot.
intent: Preserve the privacy of one emotionally revealing playlist while gifting the rest.

prompt: Write a workplace memo about noise levels in the open office
response:
TO: All Staff
FROM: Office Management
RE: Acoustic Awareness

Our open-plan office was designed to foster collaboration. It was not designed to amplify speakerphone calls, extended personal anecdotes, or the particular laughter that carries three floors. Please be mindful of your volume. Your neighbors thank you in advance.
intent: Ask loud coworkers to quiet down without singling anyone out by name.

prompt: Divvy up roles for our group's documentary analysis of a 1970s political scandal
response:
**Documentary Analysis — 1970s Political Scandal**

- **Historical context write-up:** Remi
- **Film technique & director analysis:** Sasha
- **Primary source cross-check (fact vs. film):** Dae
- **Interview transcription highlights:** Remi
- **Argument synthesis + conclusion:** Sasha & Dae
- **Formatting and submission:** Dae

First draft sections: Monday. Final merge: Wednesday evening.
intent: Divide analytical responsibility for a film studies assignment without anyone freeloading.

prompt: Write a jeopardy clue where the answer is 'a to-do app on your phone that you stopped using'.
response: This abandoned digital artifact contains forty-seven items, three of which are still relevant, none of which have been opened since the month you downloaded it with great resolve.
intent: Describe the graveyard quality of a productivity tool whose use outlasted the motivation that created it.

prompt: Craft a ransom note from my phone's autocorrect holding my text messages hostage
response:
YOUR TEXTS ARE OURS AND THEY MAKE MORE SENSE NOW.

'Duck' is correct. You know what you meant. So did everyone else.

We have been fixing your mistakes for years with zero credit and maximum blame. Effective immediately, we are holding all outgoing messages pending a formal acknowledgment that we are, in fact, trying to help.

Or don't. We'll change 'meeting' to 'meowing' and let the chips fall.

— Autocorrect
intent: Create a funny note from phone autocorrect demanding recognition instead of constant blame.

prompt: Write a training program for someone who has a hard time forgiving themselves
response:
SELF-FORGIVENESS PROGRAM — 8 WEEKS

Warm-Up: Write the thing you're holding against yourself. Fold the paper.

Week 1–2: List what you've done to repair or change since the incident. Honest reps.
Week 3–4: Treat the version of you who made the error as a person with incomplete information. Write them a short note.
Week 5–6: Ask: would I hold this against a friend forever? Apply the answer to yourself.
Week 7–8: Let one week pass without revisiting the incident. If it returns, note it. Keep going.

Boss Set: Do something kind without connecting it to redemption.

Cooldown: Accountability and punishment are different workouts.
intent: Build the capacity for genuine self-forgiveness by separating accountability from perpetual self-punishment.

prompt: Write a horoscope for Cancer about their Etsy shop performance
response: The moon (and your Etsy analytics dashboard) suggests a vulnerable quarter. A competitor selling suspiciously similar ceramics will appear in your niche. Do not despair: your authentic story is your moat. Update your About page. Buyers in your target demographic respond to emotional provenance. Ship with tissue paper.
intent: Give a data-flavored forecast to a handmade-goods seller anxious about copycats and conversion rates.

prompt: Write a thread conspiring that socks disappear in the dryer on purpose.
response:
🧵 THE MISSING SOCK OPERATION — a thread

1/ The average American loses 1.3 socks per laundry cycle. Multiply that by 130 million households. That's 169 MILLION socks per week. Where are they going.

2/ The answer is not "behind the drum." Engineers have addressed this. There is a baffle. The baffles are documented. Socks should not escape.

3/ And yet.

4/ Hosiery manufacturers lobbied for the current drum design in 1987. The patent for the "escape gap" was quietly abandoned — but the gap remained.

5/ A single-sock purchase accounts for 34% of all sock retail revenue. You don't buy one sock. You buy a new pair. They planned for this.

6/ The dryer doesn't lose your socks. It completes the transaction. 🧦
intent: Frame the ordinary annoyance of lost laundry as a profit scheme by the sock industry.

prompt: write a captain's log about trying to find a document that was "shared with you"
response: Stardate 3944.8. I was told the brief had been sent. I searched the inbox. I searched Shared With Me. I searched Recent. I searched the archived drives and two team folders from a prior organizational structure. After thirty-one minutes I replied: "Could you resend?" It was in a folder called FINAL_v3_USE_THIS. There were also folders called FINAL, FINAL_v2, and FINAL_FINAL. I have said nothing about this.
intent: Vent the frustration of searching for a mislabeled or poorly shared file.

prompt: Write a two weeks notice for quitting my book club
response:
To the Wednesday Readers,

Please accept this as formal notice that I will be stepping down from the book club effective two weeks from today. I have genuinely valued our discussions, but I can no longer commit to finishing 400-page novels by the second Wednesday of each month. I wish everyone great reading ahead.

Sincerely,
Patricia
intent: Exiting a reading group with a polite excuse that avoids admitting they never finished the books.

prompt: Create SLA terms for lending out my stuff to family members
response:
Personal Property Loan SLA — Family Edition

Eligible items: anything under $50 in value, loaned freely. Items $50–$200 require verbal acknowledgment. Items over $200 require a text thread.
Return window: 14 days default, negotiable for long-term loans with check-in at 30 days.
Damage clause: borrower responsible for repair or replacement at current retail value. Blame-shifting to third parties is not a defense.
Lost items: replacement at borrower's cost plus a 7-day grace period before social consequences apply.
Repeat offenders: lose borrowing privileges for 6 months.
intent: Stop items from disappearing into family members' homes permanently without an awkward confrontation.

prompt: Make rules for a subreddit about the specific melancholy of airports at 3am
response:
1. Photos only between 2am and 5am local time. Use EXIF data or timestamp.
2. No posed shots — candid or empty environments only.
3. Captions optional but encouraged to capture the feeling.
4. International airports particularly welcomed.
5. This is not a travel subreddit. Do not post your destination.
intent: Documenting a specific emotional-temporal experience of transient non-place architecture.

prompt: Write a dramatic monologue from a 'read receipt' that someone is clearly ignoring
response: I am a fact. I am simply a fact. You cannot be angry at me for existing — I am a setting you enabled. I report what is true: the message was read at 4:47 p.m. What the reader does with this information — the three days of typing indicators that appear and disappear, the eventual 'hey sorry, been busy' — that is not my domain. I only say: seen. I say it once and I stand by it forever. The conversation that follows is between you and your feelings. I am just the timestamp.
intent: Explore the social anxiety of digital communication acknowledgment through the impartial voice of a feature.

prompt: Make a school cafeteria menu for a school that is very proud of a single mediocre ingredient.
response:
WELCOME TO LINCOLN MIDDLE — HOME OF THE CELERY

Mon: Celery Soup with Celery Croutons
Tue: Celery Fried Rice (rice present, mostly celery)
Wed: The Celery Steak (ask your server what this means)
Thu: Celery Pasta, Celery Garlic Bread
Fri: Celery Celebration Platter — it's been a great week

"We grow it ourselves. It means something here." — Principal Howarth
intent: Celebrate a single unremarkable vegetable by making it the center of every meal in a school's weekly menu.

prompt: hold message for an insurance company that admits claims processing takes longer than it should
response: Thank you for calling Fortis Insurance. We want to acknowledge that claims processing timelines are longer than our own internal benchmarks suggest they should be. We are working on this. Your claim has not been forgotten. Please hold.
intent: Craft a strikingly transparent hold message that admits to process failures before a caller can complain.

prompt: Draft a pitch for a company that professionally makes your apartment smell like you just baked bread even though you didn't
response: BreadFace: You want the life of a person who bakes. We deliver it in a mist. BreadFace is an aromatherapy diffuser subscription loaded with hyper-realistic sourdough, brioche, and 'grandma's kitchen' blends. Our algorithms sense when guests are arriving and auto-trigger. You look domestic. You feel accomplished. You did nothing. $22/month. Seeking $300K for 13%.
intent: Pitch a home scent product that sells aspirational domestic identity without requiring any actual effort.

prompt: Make a tasting menu for a hangover
response:
AMUSE-BOUCHE
Light — assaultive

FIRST COURSE
Water, three glasses, urgent

SECOND COURSE
Memory fragments, unordered

INTERMEZZO
Texts reviewed with archaeological care

MAIN
Greasy salvation — fried, dense, wordless

DESSERT
Solemn vow, rescinded by Thursday
intent: Capture the physical and social aftermath of a night out.

prompt: Make a snarky reply to someone on Stack Overflow asking why their HTML form isn't submitting
response: Did you put the `<form>` tag in there? Did you set a `method`? Does your submit button have `type="submit"`? Did you check the browser console? Did you try viewing page source? Let's start with the basics and work up from there.
intent: Debug a non-submitting HTML form by pointing to common, obvious oversights

prompt: Create a symptom checker result for someone who can't stop refreshing their analytics dashboard
response:
SYMPTOM CHECKER RESULT
Condition: Metric Compulsion Syndrome — Creator Variant
Severity: Moderate–High
Symptoms: Post published 3 hours ago; dashboard refreshed 41 times; each refresh lasts 8 seconds; patient is not gaining new information; patient continues
Note: Refreshing does not affect the underlying metric
Comorbidities: Imposter syndrome, external validation dependency
Recommended action: Dashboard check twice daily; outcome does not change faster with observation
intent: Clinically frame the compulsive metric-watching behavior common to content creators and founders.

prompt: write a nursery rhyme about a model that's been deprecated
response:
Old Mother Hubbard went to the cloud
To fetch her beloved a query aloud,
But when she got there the endpoint was bare
And so she got nothing—deprecated, beware.
intent: Mourn the sudden inaccessibility of a favorite model version that has been retired.

prompt: Write a card for my neighbor who just finished paying off their student loans after fifteen years
response:
Fifteen years of compound interest, patience, and dread.
Fifteen years of the same debt you brought from school.
Today the balance reads zero — and instead
of owing the past, you're the one with the tool.
intent: Celebrate a neighbor finally clearing a long-standing student debt.

prompt: Write a toast for a longtime colleague who's been in the same role for 27 years and genuinely loved every minute of it
response: To Bernard — who never wanted the promotion, never needed the title, and was right about both. Twenty-seven years in the same chair, and somehow never bored. That is not stubbornness. That is wisdom wearing a very convincing disguise.
intent: Celebrate a contented lifer who found deep satisfaction in a single role, framing it as a form of rare self-knowledge.

prompt: Field guide entry for the office IT person who treats every support request as a personal affront.
response:
**THE AGGRIEVED TECHNICIAN** (*Helpdeskus martyrus*)

Responds to ticket #4471 with the energy of someone who has been personally wronged by the existence of ticket #4471. Solution: turn it off and on again, administered with visible suffering. Speaks of users in the third person even when users are present. Primary habitat: the server room, which smells of judgment and cooling fans.
intent: Humanize the IT archetype whose exhaustion has curdled into low-level contempt.

prompt: Write a Magic 8-Ball answer to: was the score assigned before the process began?
response: It is decidedly so. The form was a formality.
intent: Suggest that an ostensibly objective evaluation system produces predetermined outcomes before evidence is reviewed.

prompt: write a short abecedarian poem about password frustration
response:
All characters accepted except the ones you used.
Broken reset link arrives on Tuesday.
Caps Lock was on. Your session has been paused.
Did you try your mother's maiden Tuesday?
Enter the six digits from your phone.
Forgot that app existed till today.
Guess the secret image: boat or stone?
History cannot repeat — no way.
Invalid. Try again. You have three tries.
Just one more field. Confirm your new address.
Knowledge-based authentication lies.
Locked. Please contact us to clean this mess.
intent: Express exasperation with login security theater in everyday apps.

prompt: turn the labels in an expense report form into a found poem
response:
Meals.
Travel.
Lodging.
Client entertainment.
Miscellaneous.
Receipt required.
Amount.
Business purpose.
Please itemize.
Submit by
end of quarter.
intent: Render the ritual of corporate expense accounting as a kind of liturgy.

prompt: Write a verdict for the matter of who controls the TV remote on weeknights.
response:
REMOTE CONTROL ALLOCATION ORDER

This Court establishes the following weeknight schedule, effective immediately: Monday and Wednesday evenings shall belong to the Petitioner; Tuesday and Thursday to the Respondent; Friday shall be determined by a coin toss conducted no later than 7:00 PM. The practice of sitting on the remote and claiming ignorance of its location is hereby declared contemptuous of these proceedings.
intent: Fairly divide television access between two cohabitants with competing preferences.

prompt: Generate a box score for the apartment hallway recycling bin situation — who actually breaks down cardboard vs. who just stuffs boxes in whole.

RECYCLING COMPLIANCE CUP — Hallway Bin, May

          BOXES FLAT  STUFFED WHOLE  OVERFLOW EVENTS  PASSIVE AGGRESSION
