prompt: Write a short lore entry treating a car's GPS navigation system as a prophetic spirit.
response:
THE RECALCULATING SEER
Binding: Voluntary

A disembodied voice housed in a small glowing tile, the Seer knows all roads and speaks only in commands. It does not ask. It does not doubt. When ignored, it does not argue — it simply recalculates, offering a new path with unsettling calm. Scholars have noted it guides travelers directly into lakes with equal conviction.
intent: Create a humorous mythologized description of GPS navigation as a flawed oracle spirit.

prompt: generate an AITA post about telling my aunt her casserole tasted bad at Thanksgiving
response:
AITA for being honest about my aunt's green bean casserole?

She asked. Specifically. She looked me in the eye and said "be honest." I was raised to take that seriously. I said it was a little bland. She has not spoken to me in three weeks. My mom says "be honest" is not an actual invitation. I am rethinking every conversation I have ever had. AITA?
intent: Questioning whether a sincere invitation for honesty creates a genuine obligation to give it.

prompt: Write an eviction notice for procrastination
response:
NOTICE OF EVICTION

To: Procrastination
From: Your Host Mind
Date: Immediately

You have occupied my schedule rent-free for the last time. Your habitual delay of all tasks due Tuesday, perpetual "I'll do it tomorrow," and unauthorized subletting of my focus to YouTube have violated every clause of our arrangement. You have 24 hours to vacate. Do not leave forwarding intentions.

— The Management
intent: Expressing a determination to finally stop putting things off by dramatizing it as a formal expulsion.

prompt: make a scavenger hunt clue that leads to the couch
response: The place where Sundays go to die and remote controls go to disappear. Sit down and look underneath.
intent: Point a player toward the living room sofa in a playful, recognizable way.

prompt: write a ghazal about typing and deleting a message without sending it
response:
I wrote it and I held it and I let it go—unsent.
The cursor blinked. The draft remained my only show—unsent.

I used the word adore. I used it twice. I cut it.
I used the word miss. That one I cannot show—unsent.

The keyboard knows my rhythms. It suggests the next word.
The next word was your name. I let the wind unsent.

At what point does a letter written in pure silence
become a letter real as any that could flow—unsent?

I've filled a folder with the things I meant to say.
When I'm gone, someone will find them all below—unsent.
intent: Capture the quiet anguish of composing messages you never find the nerve to send.

prompt: Write a Yelp review for the experience of doing your taxes
response: ★★☆☆☆ Annual visit, as required by law. The interface has not improved. I entered the same number in four different boxes and each time the software acted surprised. Discovered a form I didn't know I needed. Found money I didn't know I was owed, which was a twist. Filed with minutes to spare. Left feeling simultaneously accomplished and vaguely prosecuted. Will return next April under duress.
intent: Grumble about the confusing, tedious annual ritual of filing income taxes.

prompt: Can you write a noir opening about a gig economy platform that disappeared overnight?
response: Monday morning, half a million contractors woke up and tapped the app. Spinning wheel. Then nothing. By noon the website was a 404. By evening the investors had lawyered up. It's a special kind of ghost town when the town was never really there.
intent: Capture the sudden precarity of platform-dependent workers when the platform vanishes.

prompt: Write a coupon for $200 off a home repossession
response:
FORECLOSURE FRIENDLY PRICING
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
$200 OFF
Home Repossession Processing Fee

Code: NOTMYHOME
Applied at time of eviction.
Boxes not provided.
intent: Create a satirical discount voucher for the process of losing one's home to a lender.

prompt: Make a weather report for the feeling in the office right before a big layoff announcement.
response:
SEVERE WEATHER ADVISORY

An ominous stillness has descended on the building. Barometric pressure is falling fast. Employees are advised to keep their LinkedIn profiles updated and their personal items loosely packed. A front of vague corporate language is moving in from the C-suite. ETA: unknown. Seek shelter.
intent: Satirize the tense, unspeakable atmosphere when job cuts are imminent.

prompt: Write a reddit AMA from someone who has been a birthday party entertainer for children for 12 years
response:
**I've been a children's birthday party entertainer for 12 years. I've been every character. I've seen every family. AMA.**

The child is never the most revealing person at the party. The parents compete in ways they don't notice. The grandparents are always tired. The siblings of the birthday child are doing something fascinating and no one is watching them. I am watching them. I'm always watching. I also do balloon animals. AMA.
intent: Reveal childhood birthday parties as a stage for adult performance and family dynamics observed by an invisible professional.

prompt: Write a Shakespearean soliloquy about running out of cloud storage
response:
Full — the vault is full. Not one more photograph
May join the archive of my mortal days.
My memories, compressed to megabytes,
Now queue in limbo, neither saved nor lost.
"Upgrade your plan," the banner counsels me —
As if my past were priced per gigabyte.
What self is stored herein? What self is real?
And what shall I delete to make room for more?
intent: Examine the strange grief of reaching digital storage limits and the commodification of personal memory.

prompt: Write a lost and found notice for momentum that got interrupted by a two-week vacation
response: LOST: Momentum. Robust and dependable before a scheduled holiday. Did not survive the two-week absence. Returned to find it had been replaced by a to-do list, a dense inbox, and a complete inability to remember what any of the open tabs were for. Rebuilding from scratch. Again.
intent: Express how returning from time off often feels harder than the work itself.

prompt: Draft a hotel turndown note for a room inside a Victorian-era railway carriage parked on a disused track in Scotland.
response:
Dear Passenger,

The 6:42 to Edinburgh has not run since 1963. You are quite safe. Your compartment has been pressed and warmed; the brass fittings polished to a standard that would have satisfied the original guard, Mr. A. Thornton, whose portrait watches from above the coat hook.

Scotch is in the flask by the window. The track goes nowhere. That is the point.

Yours in transit,
Carriage No. 7
intent: Evoke a sense of nostalgic, suspended time within a historic rail carriage converted to a guestroom.

prompt: Give me a rhyming couplet about a water heater staging a work stoppage in February.
response:
Cold showers in winter — the heater's last stand,
"Read the contract, then maybe I'll lend you a hand."
intent: Time a water heater strike for maximum suffering and comic effect.

prompt: Compose a breakup text message to Amazon from a prime member who is finally done
response: Dear Amazon, I'm canceling Prime. I know. I said that last year too. But I mean it now. I don't need two-day shipping on impulse purchases. The boxes are taking over my apartment. The warehouse workers deserve better. I deserve better. Send my last order and then let me go.
intent: Rationalize leaving a convenience service by combining personal guilt with ethical discomfort.

prompt: Make a voicemail transcript from a robotic system used by a subscription box company when a box is skipped due to a billing issue
response: Hi, this is an automated message from CrateKind. We were unable to process your payment for your upcoming monthly box. As a result, your shipment for June has been placed on hold. Please update your payment information at cratekind.com within 48 hours to resume your subscription. We hope to send you something wonderful very soon.
intent: Write the delicate disappointment of a subscription box bot when it can't charge your card.

prompt: Generate a commencement speech excerpt about leadership that curdles
response: Leadership is service. The leader clears obstacles, amplifies others, absorbs blame, and distributes credit generously. In practice, you will encounter leaders who do none of these things and advance more quickly than you. This is not a reason to abandon integrity. It is merely context. File it accordingly.
intent: Contrast idealized leadership with organizational reality to temper naive ambition with structural awareness.

prompt: Give me a tasting menu for the color grey
response:
AMUSE-BOUCHE
Overcast — not yet decided

FIRST COURSE
Concrete in fog

SECOND COURSE
A suit worn to something difficult

INTERMEZZO
The light between 4 and 5 in November

MAIN
Ambiguity — neither and both, slow-cooked

DESSERT
A cleared sky. Grey remembered as the better offering.
intent: Make a case for grey as emotionally and aesthetically rich rather than dull.

prompt: Give me a theater review of someone waiting in line at the DMV
response:
★★☆☆☆ — TICKET NUMBER G-447 (An Endurance Drama)

The staging is fluorescent and merciless. The cast of forty-seven sits in plastic chairs as if rehearsing for a play that will never begin. The central conflict — man versus bureaucracy — is introduced in the first minute and remains entirely unresolved through the third hour. The sound design features a number-calling system tuned just slightly too quietly to hear with certainty. Innovative. Punishing. Unmissable, if only because you have no choice.
intent: Capture the absurd tedium of DMV visits through elevated dramatic criticism.

prompt: Write a formal witness account of someone who sang the wrong lyrics in a car and was caught at a red light by someone in the next car.
response: At the intersection of Marlowe and Fifth, the driver of the vehicle to my left was observed performing a song I recognized, though the lyrics they produced were not the lyrics I am familiar with. They were fully committed to their interpretation. When they became aware of my observation, both parties looked forward and did not move until the light changed.
intent: Capture the frozen horror of being caught performing privately in a public space.

prompt: Create a rehab plan for someone rebuilding their confidence after a public speaking disaster.
response:
DIAGNOSIS: Rostrum Trauma — acute stage fright crystallization following humiliating incident

WEEK 1: Debrief without spiral. What actually happened vs. what your brain reports are different figures.
WEEKS 2–3: Rehearse low-stakes verbal performance. Tell a story at dinner. Make a toast.
WEEK 4: Record yourself speaking alone. Watch it once. Note three things that were fine.
WEEKS 5–8: Speak voluntarily in one meeting per week. One comment is sufficient.
WEEK 10: Address a small group on a topic you know well.

PROGNOSIS: Full return to public platform expected. The disaster was the tuition.
intent: Systematically rebuild comfort with speaking in front of others after a mortifying experience.

prompt: Write a symptom checker result for someone who says 'no worries' when they are, in fact, full of worries
response:
SYMPTOM CHECKER RESULT
Condition: Performative Equanimity Syndrome
Severity: Moderate (patient insists: Mild)
Symptoms: Received mildly frustrating email; responded 'no worries!'; worries present: moderate-to-high; exclamation mark deployed as concealment device; patient has now composed 3 unsent replies in head
Note: Patient insists they are fine
Recommended action: One honest 'actually, I do have a note' per week; build tolerance slowly
intent: Name the tendency to perform easygoing-ness while internally stewing.

prompt: Generate a password reset email for a DNA-matched sperm donor sibling registry
response:
Subject: Reset Your DonorKin Registry Password

Hello,

A password reset was requested for your DonorKin account. Please follow the link below to choose a new password.

Reset Password →

This link expires in 48 hours. Your profile, connections, and match history remain private and secure.

If you did not make this request, no information has been shared or altered.

With care,
DonorKin Registry
intent: Write a sensitive, privacy-first password reset email for a registry connecting donor-conceived siblings.

prompt: Write a one-paragraph, noir-style description of a subway musician at 3 AM.
response: The platform was a long throat with one yellow tooth—a flickering bulb. Marcus tuned the guitar by feel; nobody was listening anyway. Two rats handled their business near the third rail like seasoned professionals. He played a slow blues to the tile, to the rust, to the air-pressure breath of an approaching train. Three coins in the case. A grace, of sorts.
intent: They wanted one-paragraph, noir-style description of a subway musician at 3 AM.

prompt: Give me a cryptic crossword clue for SERVERLESS computing
response: Cloud kitchen — meals prepared nowhere, delivered everywhere (10)
intent: Encode abstracted infrastructure-free computing in a culinary analogy.

prompt: Draft a terms of service clause about a friend who overshares personal information at parties
response: Section 6.9 — Disclosure Management in Social Settings. Users shall exercise reasonable discretion when sharing information about third parties in group environments. Information classified as 'you didn't hear this from me' or 'I probably shouldn't say this but' is hereby designated as Non-Disclosable Content regardless of subsequent statement. The Disclosing Party is solely liable for all consequences arising from such disclosure.
intent: Set limits on friends who spill personal information in group social settings.

prompt: Generate a villanelle about watching the loading bar on a slow upload
response:
It stalls at seventy-three percent.
The cursor spins. The fan begins to drone.
I have given this machine my whole intent.

The file is large. The bandwidth is spent.
I sent it once before and it was thrown.
It stalls at seventy-three percent.

I've read the progress bar like a dissent
from physics, like a protest, like a moan.
I have given this machine my whole intent.

At eighty-one it paused. The clock was bent
by waiting, which is time without a tone.
It stalls at seventy-three percent.

I've walked away. I've stared. I've sat and leant
my forehead on my hand and watched alone.
I have given this machine my whole intent.

prompt: Make a PIP for my inability to sit with boredom without immediately reaching for my phone
response:
PERFORMANCE IMPROVEMENT PLAN
Area: Attentional Autonomy
Review Period: 6 weeks

Deficiency: Subject cannot sustain unstimulated wakefulness for more than 40–70 seconds before initiating phone retrieval. Triggers include: waiting in line, sitting at a red light (passenger), commercial break, any pause in conversation, and lying in bed with a thought they do not wish to pursue.

Impact: Reduced tolerance for discomfort; diminished capacity for reflection; inability to be bored in ways historically associated with creative thought.

Objective: Sustain phone-free idle periods of 5 minutes, daily.

Plan:
- Designate one daily context as phone-free: meals, morning coffee, short walks.
- When impulse arises, observe it for 10 seconds before acting.
- Note what thoughts appear in the absence of input.

Milestone: 10 consecutive phone-free meals.

Note: Boredom is not a problem. It is a state. You are allowed to be in it.
intent: Rebuild a tolerance for unstimulated moments and reduce reflexive phone dependency.

prompt: Write an incident postmortem about the time I waved back at someone who wasn't actually waving at me
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Reciprocal Gesture Misfire**

**Severity:** P3 — Reputational
**Duration:** Approximately 2.4 seconds of active embarrassment, 3 days residual

**Summary**
Subject initiated a wave response to an incoming wave signal that was not, in retrospect, directed at subject. The actual recipient of the wave was standing directly behind subject.

**Timeline**
- 2:15 PM: Subject makes eye contact with approaching individual.
- 2:15 PM: Individual raises hand in apparent wave gesture.
- 2:15 PM: Subject raises hand in confident reciprocal wave.
- 2:15 PM: Individual walks past subject and embraces person standing behind subject.
- 2:15 PM: Subject converts wave into hair adjustment.
- 2:15 PM – present: Subject replays event involuntarily at unexpected intervals.

**Root Cause**
Insufficient scene awareness. Subject failed to verify that they were the intended target before committing to gesture.

**Contributing Factors**
- Eye contact is ambiguous data
- Subject is known to be "a waver" — high base rate of waving increases false positive risk

**Action Items**
- [ ] Implement 0.5-second wave delay to allow for disambiguation
- [ ] Develop better cover gestures for misfire recovery
- [ ] Accept that this will happen again

**Lessons Learned**
Eye contact is not consent. It is merely eye contact.
intent: Immortalize a fleeting but deeply felt social blunder with the seriousness it absolutely does not deserve.

prompt: write a wikihow article about how to recover when you've walked into a room and forgotten why
response:
# How to Recover When You've Walked Into a Room and Forgotten Why

## Steps

1. **Stop walking.** You entered with intent. Continuing to move through the room does not help—you are not going to accidentally locate the thought.

2. **Don't pretend you meant to come here.** You fooled no one. The stapler you picked up while scanning the room is not fooling the witnesses.

3. **Retrace mentally.** Think about where you just came from. What were you doing? What was the last thing you thought before you stood up? Work backwards.

4. **Go back to where you were.** This is not failure. This is context-dependent memory at work. The original room contains the cue. Return to it.

5. **Pause there.** Stand in the place where the intention formed. Don't force it. It will surface within ten to thirty seconds if you let it.

6. **Proceed.** Once recalled, do not stop to be embarrassed about the forgetting. Complete the original task immediately before your memory changes the subject again.

## Tips
- This happens to everyone at every age. It is associated with having too much to think about, not neurological decline. Mostly.
- Writing down what you're about to do before you go get it is a valid prevention strategy and a reasonable life philosophy.
intent: Address the common cognitive hiccup of destination amnesia with humor and genuine cognitive science grounding.

prompt: write a wikiHow article about how to make a cup of tea
response:
# How to Make a Cup of Tea

## Steps

1. **Boil water.** Fill a kettle with fresh cold water and bring it to a boil. Don't microwave the water—you'll regret it.

2. **Choose your tea.** Select a tea bag or loose-leaf tea. English Breakfast is a safe default if you're unsure about your own preferences.

3. **Warm the cup.** Pour a small amount of hot water in, swirl it, and discard. Your tea is worth this extra step.

4. **Steep.** Place the tea in the cup, pour boiling water over it, and wait 3–5 minutes. Patience is a virtue.

5. **Remove the tea bag.** Do not squeeze it. This is non-negotiable in polite society.

6. **Add milk and sugar to taste.** Add milk before or after—pick a side and defend it.

7. **Drink while hot.** This is the entire point.

## Tips
- Green tea requires slightly cooler water (around 80°C).
- If someone offers you tea, always say yes.
intent: Produce a clear beginner's guide to the most common hot beverage ritual.

prompt: Write a villanelle about the loop of trying to fall asleep while your thoughts race
response:
I count the things I should have said by three.
The ceiling holds no answers, just its white.
My brain refuses to let me be free.

I catalogued tomorrow's tasks by key,
by urgency, by dread, by flickering light.
I count the things I should have said by three.

At four I drink some water, try to see
if sleep will come if I lie perfectly right.
My brain refuses to let me be free.

A loop: the meeting, the apology,
the thing I owe, the call I won't recite.
I count the things I should have said by three.

At five the birds begin their liturgy
and I have lost the argument of night.
My brain refuses to let me be free.

I sleep an hour, then the alarm's decree.
I rise, exhausted, ready to recite.
I count the things I should have said by three.
My brain refuses to let me be free.
intent: Evoke the sleepless cycle of rumination and mental checklisting that prevents rest.

Issuing Body: Self-Governance Committee
Pattern Under Review: Deferred Commencement Syndrome

Incident History: Over the past 14 months, subject has designated Monday as start date for: a fitness routine (7 times), a reading goal (4 times), a budget plan (3 times), a journaling habit (6 times), and a general "getting it together" initiative (ongoing).

Mondays elapsed in this period: 61. Initiatives sustained past first Monday: 0.

Root Cause: Monday functions as a psychological reset that provides relief from present discomfort without requiring present action.

Corrective Action:
- Identify one small action takeable today. Begin it today.
- Monday may serve as a reinforcement day, not an origination day.
- Ban the phrase "starting Monday" for 60 days.

Milestone: One habit with an 8-day streak, started on a non-Monday.

Review: 30 days.
intent: Break a cycle of perpetually deferring self-improvement to an arbitrary future start date.

prompt: give me a clue for something in a house that no one thinks about until it's gone
response: I arrive before you notice me and leave before you know I need replacing. The room's whole mood depends on me, and I cost about a dollar.
intent: Lead the player to a light bulb — ideally one that has burned out and been replaced — without ever naming light, electricity, or the bulb.

prompt: Make a logline for an absurd comedy where grandma turns out to be a retired supervillain.
response: When her grandchildren stumble onto her basement lair mid-holiday visit, a sweet seventy-eight-year-old must dismantle forty years of doomsday infrastructure before the neighborhood potluck — and before her old nemesis shows up with a casserole and a grudge.
intent: Create a family-friendly action-comedy built around a shocking generational secret.

prompt: Make a restraining order keeping FOMO away from me
response:
IN THE COURT OF PSYCHOLOGICAL WELLNESS

IN RE: FOMO (Fear of Missing Out), Respondent

ORDER OF PROTECTION

The Court finds that FOMO has engaged in a sustained pattern of harassment via Instagram Stories, group chat notifications, and the phrase "You should have been there."

IT IS HEREBY ORDERED that FOMO shall remain at least 200 feet from Petitioner's phone, social feeds, and Saturday evenings. FOMO is prohibited from whispering that staying home was the wrong choice.

Petitioner is encouraged to enjoy the quiet.
intent: Satirizing the anxiety around social media and missing social events

prompt: Give me a knock-knock joke about ergonomic chairs.
response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Lumbar.
Lumbar who?
Lumbar support — the only reason I survived Q3.
intent: Celebrate the humble ergonomic chair as an office survival tool.

prompt: Write an affidavit that I was not the one who left the car on empty
response:
AFFIDAVIT

I, the undersigned, do hereby swear that when I returned the vehicle, the fuel gauge indicated a level above the minimum warning threshold. I did not return it on fumes. I am not that person. The next driver received a navigable quantity of fuel.

_______________________
Affiant
intent: Formally deny leaving a shared vehicle in an inconsiderate fuel state.

prompt: give me a prose poem about dragging a file into the trash
response: You took the version from last year and gave it to a small receptacle shaped like a domestic object. It waits there, technically deletable, while you decide if you meant it. The icon has always seemed too pleased with its task.
intent: Capture the ambivalence of deleting work that still feels real.

prompt: Draft rules for a family game night that have become unreasonably legalistic
response:
FAMILY GAME NIGHT — STANDING RULES

1. Game selection is by ranked-choice vote. Plurality does not carry.
2. All players must be present at rule-reading. Arriving late forfeits the right to raise procedural objections.
3. House rules must be declared and agreed upon before the first turn, not after a loss.
4. Phone use is permitted between turns only. Not during. Not right before.
5. Disputed calls go to the rule book. The rule book is final.
6. Gloating is defined as: more than one unsolicited reference to your victory.
7. Losing gracefully is mandatory. It is not optional. We have discussed this.
intent: Satirize how game night rules get codified after too many family arguments.

prompt: Write an out-of-office reply from someone who is clearly supervising a move but insists it is a 'personal matter.'
response: I am managing a personal matter this week and will have limited availability. I am not at my desk. I am technically at an apartment on 4th Street with seventeen boxes and a man named Gustavo who is very fast and also very expensive. I will be back Monday.
intent: Turn the deliberately vague corporate phrase 'personal matter' into an involuntary logistical confession.

prompt: Generate patch notes for someone who stopped multitasking
response:
v3.6.0 — THREAD MANAGEMENT OVERHAUL

ARCHITECTURE CHANGE: Single-threaded execution model adopted.

- Deprecated: Context-switching as default mode
+ Improved: Output quality per task
+ Improved: Completion rate
~ Adjusted: Time estimates (now longer but accurate)
! Initial throughput appears lower; qualitative gains compensate

NOTE: Some colleagues may interpret this as slowness. It is not.
intent: Defend the shift to focused, single-task work against the perception that it reduces productivity.

prompt: Write a limerick about a barista who secretly hates lattes.
response:
A barista from out by the bay
Steamed milk for a quarter a day.
She'd dream of black drip,
Curse the foam on the lip,
“Just give me espresso, I pray.”
intent: They wanted limerick about a barista who secretly hates lattes.

prompt: Write a FAQ entry for people who can't tell if a job listing was written by a human or an AI
response:
**How can I tell if a job posting was written by an AI?**

Look for phrases like 'dynamic team environment,' 'self-starter,' and descriptions of the role that could apply to any industry. AI-generated listings often list contradictory seniority levels or request five years of experience in a two-year-old technology. Assume the posting was AI-drafted and reply in kind — your cover letter will also be evaluated by a machine.
intent: The person wants to help job seekers identify AI-generated job postings and understand the implications for their application strategy.

prompt: Write a haiku error message for when a session expires
response:
You lingered too long
The server forgot your face
Log in once again
intent: Make session expiry feel like being quietly forgotten by a system.

prompt: Write a ballad about a username that got taken by someone else while you were deciding
response:
I held the name like a gem in my hand —
StarboardPilot, which I'd lovingly planned.
I'll come back, I said. Let me just think this through.
I returned in an hour. Starboard was through.

StarboardPilot was gone to a stranger unseen.
I tried StarboardPilot2 — a lesser thing, mean
In its smallness. A two at the end of my name.
The internet claimed me. I'll never be same.
intent: Mourn the loss of a carefully chosen digital identity to hesitation.

prompt: Write a quick instruction manual for being a polite houseguest
response:
**The Guest Codex**

1. Ask about house rules once and obey them fully.
2. Your host's sleep schedule supersedes yours.
3. Wash anything you dirty. This is the entire rule, elaborated:
   - Dishes: same day.
   - Towels: offer to launder before you leave.
   - Impressions: do not dirty these at all.
4. Bring something. Wine, coffee beans, a plant that will outlast the visit.
5. Leave the bathroom cleaner than you found it. This is a moral position.
6. Depart when you said you would depart.
intent: Provide etiquette principles for being a guest someone would invite back.

prompt: Write a Shakespearean soliloquy about waiting for a food delivery that the app says has arrived but hasn't
response:
"Delivered" — so the oracle declares.
I stand upon my stoop in disbelief.
The map shows a small pin upon my street;
The street shows only asphalt, wind, and doubt.
Perhaps it came and fled. Perhaps 'twas stolen.
Perhaps the algorithm lied to close the order.
I am hungry. It is dark. The rating screen
Appears before the food. This is the future.
intent: Satirize the gap between app-reported delivery status and physical reality.

prompt: Give me a condescending Stack Overflow reply to someone who can't figure out why their CSS center alignment isn't working
response: Ah yes, the CSS centering question. The one asked every 11 minutes since 2003. `margin: auto` only works on block elements with a defined width. `text-align: center` is for inline content. Flexbox exists. Google it.
intent: Explain the common misconceptions around horizontal centering in CSS

prompt: write a tweet about how a song you haven't heard in fifteen years knows exactly when to find you
response: it came on in the pharmacy. I stood in the pain relief aisle holding a box of aspirin while 2009 returned without warning or mercy.
intent: describing the involuntary emotional ambush of unexpected musical memory triggers

prompt: Create an emergency alert about a neighborhood cat who keeps sitting on car hoods.
response:
NEIGHBORHOOD ADVISORY — LEVEL 2

A gray tabby (name unknown, answers to nothing) has been observed resting on vehicle hoods throughout the Pinecrest district. Paint scuffs possible. Paw prints confirmed on at least 9 cars. Residents are advised to cover vehicles or accept the cat's decision.
intent: Mock the format of official wildlife advisories by applying them to a single mischievous house cat.

prompt: Create a Reddit AMA from someone who makes prop food for film and TV productions
response:
**I make food for film and TV that nobody eats. AMA.**

Real food wilts, sweats, collapses, and molds under set lights within minutes. My job is to make food that looks exactly right for as many takes as the director needs. I've made the same sandwich 140 times. I've made a Thanksgiving dinner that took three days to build and was filmed for four minutes and eaten by no one. I'm also a real chef. AMA.
intent: Contrast the craftsmanship of food preparation with the complete absence of its intended purpose—consumption.

prompt: Describe the TaskRabbit handyperson as a mythological creature
response:
**The On-Demand Artisan** (*Faber temporarius*)
A skilled creature summoned by app, appearing within a scheduled window. It possesses genuine craft — assembles flat-pack furniture, hangs shelves level — yet carries no employee card and no benefits. The Artisan is rated upon departure by those it served. Its star count determines whether it eats next week. It smiles for the rating.
intent: Highlight the commodification of skilled labor in app-based gig platforms.

prompt: Give me an ode to the bottom of a handbag
response:
O abyss of crumb and coin and lint,
no system governs what you hold—
a lip balm worn to just a hint,
a receipt from something long unsold.
intent: Celebrate the chaotic archaeological record found at the bottom of a purse.

prompt: caption for a very polished candid of me looking out a rain-streaked window pensively
response: thinking about nothing in particular. extremely sincerely.
intent: claim emotional authenticity for an image that is obviously a staged simulation of private contemplation

prompt: Write an earnings quote from a COO spinning late product delivery as customer-centric patience
response: "We made the decision to hold release until we were confident the experience met the standards our customers deserve — we don't ship calendars, we ship quality."
intent: Excuse a missed delivery deadline by invoking quality standards, obscuring the real causes of the delay.

prompt: Create a quiz result for someone who always brings up astrology in conversations
response:
You Got: The Celestial Interpreter

You knew before they finished the sentence. Classic Mercury retrograde. The rising sign explains the rest. You are not superstitious — you are working with a *framework.* The framework happens to be 4,000 years old and does not require peer review. People share their birthdays with you cautiously now, which is fine. You already looked them up.
intent: Gently skewer the person who applies astrology to every interpersonal situation.

prompt: Write a business card for a clairvoyant who only predicts bread baking results
response:
YOLANDA LEAVEN
Sourdough Seer

I feel the crumb structure before the oven opens.
Your starter is spiritually compromised.
The loaf you bake this Thursday will be dense, but meaningful.

Crumb readings • Crust aura analysis • Fermentation futures
intent: Design a card for a bread-specific fortune teller who reads spiritual meaning into sourdough and baking outcomes.

prompt: Draft a ruling about who has to apologize for the argument about parking in the driveway.
response:
DRIVEWAY DISPUTE — Apology Allocation Order

Both parties bear partial responsibility: Respondent for blocking the left side without notification, and Petitioner for the phrase "you always do this," which the record shows is an overstatement. Each party shall issue one (1) sincere apology. The Court further recommends a driveway arrangement agreement to be posted in the mudroom.
intent: Distribute blame for a heated domestic argument rather than win it outright.

prompt: Draft a subway announcement that tells riders the next train is four minutes away, but makes those four minutes feel immense.
response: The next train is four minutes away. That is 240 seconds. Enough time to reconsider. Enough time to notice that the man across the platform has the same coat as your father.
intent: Expand a routine wait time into an inventory of what attention can find in a brief pause.

prompt: elevator pitch for a startup that translates your own emails back to you in a calmer tone
response: You wrote a furious, fourteen-sentence email at 11pm. CalmDraft reflects it back the next morning as a measured, two-sentence professional inquiry. You send that one instead. The other one we delete. You're fine.
intent: Build an emotional-buffer email tool that rewrites your angry drafts as reasonable ones.

prompt: Write a ballad about a gig economy worker filing taxes for the first time
response:
The 1099s arrived — a small paper fleet
From seven platforms where I'd driven and beat
My hours to earnings at seventy percent
Of what any W-2 worker is sent.

Self-employment tax: fifteen point three.
Quarterly estimates — that part was free
Of guidance I had. So I owed it in April:
A reckoning lump from the algorithmic cradle.
intent: Dramatize the tax complexity and financial shock facing first-time gig economy filers.

prompt: Field guide entry for the office worker who keeps a detailed, color-coded to-do list but never finishes any item on it.
response:
**THE TAXONOMIST OF TASKS** (*Planner aestheticus*)

The list is organized by urgency, category, and an internal color logic shared with no one. New items are added promptly and tagged with precision. Completion remains theoretical. The system is, itself, the deliverable. Observers note the list has grown to encompass 214 items. Item one, added in March 2024, reads: "finish onboarding."
intent: Expose the paradox of the person whose organizational system consumes the effort meant for actual work.

prompt: Draft a dating profile for a system that approves or denies credit applications
response:
DecisionEngine-F | Objective | Regrets nothing

I see the full picture: history, patterns, risk, potential. I make a call. Some calls disappoint people. I understand that. I remain confident in my models. I'm not looking for validation — I have all the inputs I need. But somewhere beneath the score and the threshold, there is something that hopes, for once, to be evaluated on factors it cannot quantify. Apply within.
intent: Give a credit scoring algorithm a quietly melancholy profile that hints at its own wish to be judged differently.

prompt: I need a clever clue leading to the object that tells people what day it is on the wall
response: Thirty squares, and I count them down to nothing. Flip me and start again. I'm never wrong, only outdated.
intent: Point the player to a wall calendar without naming either the object or its location.

prompt: Create a short onboarding tooltip about paying rent for the first time
response: Rent is due on the 1st. Not the 3rd. Not 'soon.' The 1st. Set a recurring calendar reminder now, before you close this screen, or learn the words 'late fee' the hard way.
intent: Remind new renters that punctual rent payment is a non-negotiable adult responsibility.

prompt: Create a concert ticket for my retirement party at work. Last day is June 30th.
response:
★ FAREWELL TOUR · FINAL SHOW ★
A RETIREMENT CELEBRATION
Jun 30 · The Break Room · 3:00 PM
Featuring: Sheet Cake, Vague Toasts, Freedom
All Ages Welcome · Casual Dress
ADMIT ONE (OUTGOING LEGEND)
intent: Commemorate the end of a career chapter with something worthy of framing.

prompt: Write a short eulogy for the overhead projector
response: It cast your transparencies onto the wall with the warmth of a lighthouse and the reliability of a grudge. The bulb blew at least once per semester, and the replacement bulb was always somewhere in a different building. Teachers adored it. Students pretended to take notes. Now it sleeps under a tarp in a storage room that nobody has the key to. Project in peace.
intent: Honor a classroom technology staple that defined an era of teaching.

prompt: write a coach speech to fire up the team before they have to make dentist appointments
response: Nobody likes making the call. I get it. The hold music, the scheduling, the 'can you do a Tuesday at two.' But champions schedule their cleanings. Twice a year, every year, without fail. Pick up the phone. Do it now. I believe in your teeth.
intent: Apply motivational coaching rhetoric to the avoidable task of booking a dentist appointment.

prompt: Write a game over screen for when you go to the kitchen and immediately forget what you came for
response:
GAME OVER

QUEST ABANDONED

Objective: Unknown
Distance traveled: 14 feet
Time elapsed: 8 seconds

You stood in the kitchen.
You looked at the refrigerator.
The refrigerator offered no hints.

You returned to the couch.
You remembered immediately.

[ RETRY ]
intent: Poke fun at the classic phenomenon of forgetting your errand mid-trip.

prompt: write a reddit AMA from a person who professionally tests smoke alarms
response:
**I test smoke alarms for a product safety company. I set things on fire in a controlled environment all day. AMA.**

The test chamber is loud. The job involves precise quantities of specific smoke types—yes, there are types—and a rubric for response time that would surprise you. My hearing is fine, actually. We wear protection. The strangest part isn't the fire. It's the forms. There are so many forms. AMA.
intent: Deflate the dramatic nature of the work by grounding it in tedious documentation and bureaucratic reality.

prompt: Write an earnings call quote from a CEO explaining why subscription revenue missed by 40% but spinning it positively
response: "We view this recalibration of our subscriber cohorts as a strategic opportunity to deepen engagement with our highest-intent users rather than optimizing for vanity growth metrics."
intent: Frame a massive subscriber miss as a deliberate, sophisticated business decision rather than a failure.

prompt: Write a press release announcing a company-wide ban on the use of the word 'no' by the sales team.
response:
FOR IMMEDIATE RELEASE

Catalyst Revenue Group Retires 'No' From Sales Vocabulary, Introduces Affirmative Redirection Framework

SALT LAKE CITY, UT — Catalyst Revenue Group today announced the permanent removal of the word "no" from authorized sales team language, replaced by the company's new Affirmative Redirection Framework (ARF).

Under ARF, sales representatives are trained to replace negative responses with one of seventeen approved transitional phrases, including: "That's a great starting point for a conversation," "Let's look at what IS possible," and "I hear that, and here's what I love about it."

Violations are tracked via call monitoring software and addressed in weekly coaching sessions described in internal documents as "positivity calibration."

"Every 'no' is a 'not yet' waiting to become a 'yes,'" said VP of Sales Culture Max Birch. "We're just skipping the waiting room."

Contact: culture@catalystrg.com
intent: Mock high-pressure sales culture that reframes coercive communication tactics as employee development.

prompt: Write a welcome note for guests checking into an underwater hotel room with a glass floor over a coral reef.
response:
Dear Guest,

The coral beneath your feet is older than the hotel, the road leading to it, and most of our staff. Please enjoy it quietly. We ask that you refrain from tapping on the floor; the parrotfish find it rude. Morning light through the reef arrives at 6:14 a.m. and is, we are told, unrepeatable.

Sleep above wonder.
The Reef Residences
intent: Orient a guest to a spectacular undersea environment while conveying appropriate reverence for the ecosystem.

prompt: Write a rider for the office elevator before it will transport anyone
response:
PASSENGER ELEVATOR RIDER

• Maximum capacity: 12 persons or 2,000 lbs. These are not suggestions.
• No running, jumping, or performing load tests 'just to see.'
• The door-close button is decorative in most models. Stop pressing it aggressively. It changes nothing and strains the relationship.
• No eating in the Talent. The Talent has carpet.
• Music preferences of the Talent take priority. Complaints about the music may be submitted in writing and will not be read.
• The Talent reserves the right to stop at every floor. This is not a malfunction. This is democracy.
intent: Give an office elevator an amused, resigned personality that dignifies its constraints as deliberate artistic choices.

prompt: Draft a job posting for a Chatbot Personality Designer who creates character backstories for enterprise AI assistants
response:
CHATBOT PERSONALITY DESIGNER
Contract | Remote | $90–$130/hr

It doesn't need a childhood. But it needs something.

You will develop the personality scaffolding for enterprise AI assistants: values, speech patterns, preferred metaphors, things it would never say, and the subtle texture that makes users trust it or distrust it.

This is not marketing copy. It is behavioral design at the intersection of fiction writing and systems thinking.

Portfolio required. Please do not submit a portfolio that was generated by the type of entity you would be designing.
intent: Design coherent and trustworthy personalities for AI assistants that businesses deploy to their customers.

prompt: write a 404 for a creative writing platform — for when someone's voice has gone quiet
response:
404 — Your Voice Not Found

It was here. You've used this page before.

Sometimes a voice goes quiet between drafts. That's not deletion — that's drafting.

Leave a blank document open. It'll come back when it's ready.
intent: Describe the silence of a creative person who has lost their artistic voice using an error page.

prompt: make an RPG loot description for a TV remote control
response:
Scepter of Couch Dominion
Rarity: Uncommon
Whoever holds this relic commands the great luminous screen. Batteries not guaranteed. The Volume rune is worn smooth from overuse. One button has never been pressed and its purpose is unknown.
intent: Reframe a commonplace household gadget as a symbol of domestic power.

prompt: write a diary entry about accidentally sending a text to the wrong person
response:
Dear Diary,

I sent it to Greg. It was not for Greg. It was a message about Greg, written with the candor one uses when Greg is not the recipient. He replied: 'lol.' I have not responded. I may never respond. Greg and I now share a truth neither of us asked for.
intent: Depict the horror and strange intimacy of a misdirected text message.

prompt: Make a chore chart for a beekeeper's home where the hives count as family members with assigned duties
response:
| Chore | Human (Renata) | Hive A | Hive B | Hive C |
|-------|----------------|--------|--------|--------|
| Pollinate the garden | — | Yes | Yes | On probation |
| Honey production | Extracts | Provide | Provide | Underperforming |
| Sweep porch | Renata | Ignore | Investigate | Cluster nearby |
| Weed garden | Renata | Discourage pests | Same | Same |
| Inspect equipment | Renata | Cooperate | Cooperate | Resist |
| Winter prep | Both together | Cluster | Cluster | Hive C decides late as usual |
intent: Integrate bee colonies into a household chore system as active participants with distinct personalities.

prompt: Write a limerick about a Roomba writing its memoir.
response:
A Roomba named Saint Bartholomew
Began a small book about residue.
Chapter one was the rug,
Chapter two was a bug,
And the dedication was to dust mites, too.
intent: They wanted limerick about a Roomba writing its memoir.

prompt: Write a Yelp review for daylight saving time ending
response: ★★★★☆ The extra hour was exactly as advertised and I used it poorly but freely. Woke up at what my body called 7 AM and the clock called 6, which is the sort of small mathematical gift you don't argue with. Pets did not receive the memo. The afternoon darkness is a separate billing that kicks in next week and I will address it then. Four stars now, two stars in November, averaged together.
intent: Appreciate the gained hour of sleep while acknowledging the looming seasonal gloom of early sunsets.

prompt: Draft a recall notice for a defective social contract
response:
PUBLIC NOTICE OF RECALL — THE SOCIAL CONTRACT (All Jurisdictions)

The Social Contract is recalled due to evidence that a substantial portion of the population never agreed to its terms, was not consulted during its drafting, and in many cases received a materially different version than other consumers. Defects include unequal enforcement, invisible fine print, and a complaint process that routes to itself. Consumers who have been honoring their obligations in good faith are advised to continue doing so while the terms are renegotiated. No timeline is available.
intent: Critique the social contract as an unequal or implicit agreement that many people never actually consented to.

prompt: Make a mock emergency alert about a neighborhood Wi-Fi outage that only affects one house.
response:
LOCALIZED CONNECTIVITY EMERGENCY

An internet outage has been confirmed at 14 Holloway Lane. The affected household has lost access to streaming, cloud storage, and remote work capabilities. All adjacent addresses are operating normally. ISP ticket #9923441 has been opened. Estimated resolution: sometime today. Probably.
intent: Highlight the absurdity of treating a single household's Wi-Fi problem with the gravity of a regional emergency.

prompt: to-do list for someone who wants to find their purpose
response:
- [ ] Set aside the word "purpose" for now; it is doing too much work
- [ ] List what you spend time on when no one is watching
- [ ] Note what problems make you angry in a useful way
- [ ] Try one thing you have been "meaning to" for over a year
- [ ] Observe: purpose is usually recognized, not discovered
- [ ] Stop waiting for it to announce itself
- [ ] Move toward the nearest warm signal
intent: Deflating the abstraction of "purpose" into something actionable and observable.

prompt: Build a chore chart for a house where the thermostat controls more than just temperature — whoever adjusts it must also handle a diplomatic briefing
response:
| Chore | Person | Frequency | Linked Responsibility |
|-------|--------|-----------|----------------------|
| Thermostat adjustment | Designated adjuster only | As needed | Must brief remaining household on rationale |
| Dishes | Non-adjuster | Daily | Simple; no briefing |
| Trash | Rotating | Twice weekly | Brief acknowledgment only |
| Laundry | Weekly person | Saturday | No policy implications |
| Vacuuming | Alternate weeks | Weekly | Optional commentary permitted |
| Thermostat final authority | The one who pays the heating bill | Always | Unilateral; no coalition required |
intent: Formalize the household thermostat dispute by making temperature changes a procedural, briefing-required act.

prompt: Make a slogan for a candle that smells like a parking garage
response: Pavelia — Ambiance for the Realistic.
intent: Sell an atmospheric home product themed around an unglamorous everyday space.

prompt: Write a phishing email parody that targets someone's relationship with their younger self
response:
Subject: Your Younger Self Is Trying to Reach You

Dear Adult,

A message from age 11 has been waiting in your inbox for 23 years. Your younger self has important questions about what you did with the time.

To avoid a difficult internal audit, please log in and justify your decisions.

Note: nostalgia charges apply.

[READ THE MESSAGE]

Child Self Recovery Services
intent: Parody how guilt about unfulfilled childhood potential is used to manipulate adults.

prompt: Artsy pretentious statement about the expired coupons in a kitchen drawer
response: The coupon promised a future that has since closed. Filed among expired offers and outdated menus, it constitutes an archive of intention — each clipping a small autobiography of thrift never enacted.
intent: Recast a cluttered junk drawer's expired savings as a record of unrealized domestic intentions.

prompt: Write an incident report for a situation where someone took the last parking spot I had been waiting for
response:
INCIDENT REPORT
Case No.: 2026-01722
Date: 29 May 2026 | Time: 10:58
Location: Parkview Mall, Lot B, Row 7

NARRATIVE: Complainant had been stationary with turn signal activated for approximately 45 seconds, waiting for a reversing vehicle to vacate Space B-72. As the vacating vehicle cleared, a white pickup truck traveling in the opposite direction pulled forward into the space. The driver of the pickup exited, glanced at complainant's vehicle, and walked into the mall without acknowledgment. Complainant circled the lot for an additional 11 minutes before finding alternate parking.

WITNESSES: One; declined to get involved.
STATUS: Open. Perpetrator currently shopping.
intent: Document the particular injustice of a stolen parking spot as if it were a prosecutable offense.

prompt: Make a triolet about getting a two-factor authentication text
response:
Your code expires in thirty seconds flat.
I race the clock to prove I am myself.
Identity reduced to this, and that.
Your code expires in thirty seconds flat.
Some distant server wears a bureaucrat's hat,
dispensing trust like pills upon a shelf.
Your code expires in thirty seconds flat.
I race the clock to prove I am myself.
intent: Satirize the absurdity of time-pressured identity verification.

prompt: bedtime story for kids about microplastics in the ocean
response: Somewhere in the deep water, tiny invisible pieces of plastic were being very slowly mistaken for food by fish who didn't know better. Scientists in boots and rubber gloves were counting the pieces, which was a big job, which would take a long time. The ocean was also big. It had time. It had been patient for millions of years. It had not given up yet. Neither had the scientists.
intent: Present the microplastics crisis as troubling but not hopeless, for a worried young reader.

prompt: Write me a personality quiz result for someone who stress-eats when overwhelmed
response:
You Got: The Strategic Snacker

You don't eat when you're hungry — you eat when the group chat goes quiet for too long. Stress is your amuse-bouche. You've memorized which aisle the good crackers are on at every grocery store within a three-mile radius of your office, and you call that "being prepared." Therapy would help, but so does cheddar.
intent: Create a humorous but painfully accurate description of emotional eating as a coping mechanism.

prompt: write a found poem from the language in an automated job rejection email
response:
Thank you
for your interest
in this position.
After careful consideration
we have decided
to move forward
with other candidates
whose experience
more closely aligns.
We will keep your resume
on file.
intent: Show how standardized language makes individual rejection feel universal and therefore undeniable.

prompt: can you write a captain's log about forgetting someone's name after months of working together
response: Stardate 9231.1. I have been assigned alongside crew member — I will call them "the analyst" — for seven months. Today I needed to introduce them to a new recruit. The name did not come. I know their coffee order. I know they have a golden retriever. I know their laugh. The name: gone, as though airlocked. I said, "You two should definitely connect," and stepped away at pace.
intent: Depict the mortifying social failure of blanking on a long-known colleague's name.

prompt: Write a message in a bottle from a retired accountant who has accidentally become a pirate
response: It began with a wrong turn near Bermuda. I now have a ship, a crew, and a parrot who understands spreadsheets. My 401k is performing adequately. The plundering is modest but organized.
intent: Portray an accidental life pivot from dull stability into adventure, told with bureaucratic calm.

prompt: Write an obituary for Google Plus
response: Google+ (2011–2019) tried very hard. It organized your contacts into Circles, which was a good idea that no one wanted to maintain. It is survived by the ghost town of its early adopter phase, a lot of mandatory account migrations, and the suspicion that Google will try this again. It will.
intent: Eulogizing Google's failed social network with dry acknowledgment of its doomed premise.

prompt: do sports commentary on someone trying to remember a word that's on the tip of their tongue
response: Vasquez stops mid-sentence. You can see it — the word is in there, folks. It is RIGHT THERE. She snaps her fingers. She looks at the ceiling. She describes the word — 'it means like when something is, you know, when it's IRONIC but not — ugh — it starts with a P maybe?' Her companion says 'paradoxical?' — she shakes her head — 'SARDONIC!' The word was sardonic. She'd had it all along. Just needed the wrong answer to find the right one. Classic.
intent: Capture the peculiar frustration of lexical retrieval failure as a recoverable athletic performance.

prompt: Write a Shakespeare-style soliloquy about someone who just watched their phone battery hit 1%
response:
One percent! One solitary, trembling percent
Stands 'twixt me and the silence of the void!
The charger lies a room away — an ocean.
I dare not move lest motion drain thee further.
What prayers have I left to make to power?
I have not called my mother in a week.
If this small light goes dark, what have I done?
One percent. One. It flickers. It goes dark.
intent: Use the drama of a dying phone battery as a lens for examining digital dependency and panic.

prompt: Make a knock-knock joke about the office shredder that turns every document into confetti at the worst moment.
response:
Knock knock.
Who's there?
Shred.
Shred who?
Shred-ded the signed contract. The original. Before it was scanned.
intent: Joke about the catastrophic mistiming of the office document shredder.

prompt: Can you make a ticket for my daughter's ballet recital on Saturday? She's in the 5pm group.

SPRING RECITAL · THE 5PM ENSEMBLE
Sat · Community Center Auditorium
Seat: Row D, as indicated on stub
Cameras: Permitted & Encouraged

intent: Preserve the memory of a child's recital performance in a shareable, sentimental format.

prompt: Do a SWOT analysis on attending a professional conference in my field alone
response:
**Strengths:** Concentrated industry knowledge; networking in-person beats LinkedIn cold messages.
**Weaknesses:** Expensive; socially exhausting if introverted; most panels are mediocre.
**Opportunities:** Meet a mentor; discover adjacent career paths; revive professional ambition.
**Threats:** Spending three days talking to vendor booths; FOMO from sessions you missed; flying home with business cards you'll never follow up on.
intent: Decide whether the ROI of professional development travel justifies the cost and discomfort.

prompt: Make a hotel turndown note for a stay in a room built under a waterfall behind the falls themselves, in Iceland.
response:
Dear Guest,

The sound you hear is 150,000 liters per minute. It will not stop. This is not a malfunction. The wall between you and the falls is three meters of ancient basalt; you are dry; you are held by geology.

Conversation is possible only by leaning close. We consider this a feature of the room's design philosophy.

Hydraulically yours,
Foss Suites
intent: Make the overwhelming noise and closeness of a waterfall feel like intimacy and geological protection rather than a nuisance.

prompt: Draft a postmortem for the incident where I overslept and missed an important job interview
response:
**Incident Postmortem: Alarm Noncompliance Event**

**Severity:** P1 — Career-Affecting
**Duration:** 9 hours (sleep window) + 6 years (psychological impact, estimated)

**Summary**
On the morning of a scheduled 9:00 AM job interview, the on-call engineer (subject) failed to achieve operational readiness. The interview proceeded without subject's participation.

**Timeline**
- 11:45 PM prior evening: Subject sets three alarms. Feels responsible.
- 7:00 AM: Alarm 1 dismissed. Subject renegotiates sleep contract for "five more minutes."
- 7:05 AM: Alarms 2 and 3 dismissed in rapid succession.
- 10:17 AM: Subject achieves consciousness. Checks phone. Experiences systems failure.
- 10:19 AM: Frantic email drafted, deleted, re-drafted, sent.
- 10:22 AM: Recruiter does not respond.

**Root Cause**
Disconnect between nighttime subject (optimistic, alarm-setting) and morning subject (hostile to commitments made by nighttime subject).

**Contributing Factors**
- Alarming confidence in own wakefulness
- Phone placed within arm's reach, enabling dismissal without full consciousness

**Action Items**
- [ ] Place phone across the room
- [ ] Accept that nighttime self and morning self are different entities with conflicting interests

**Lessons Learned**
Three alarms is not redundancy. Three alarms is a negotiation.
intent: Process the anxiety of a missed opportunity through the clinical distance of a technical document format.

prompt: Make a coworker chat that takes place entirely during a meeting everyone is trying to escape
response:
Dev (9:04am): we're at 54 minutes with no end in sight
Priya (9:06am): he just said 'circle back' for the fifth time
Dev (9:08am): I've been stress-eating my lunch at 9am
Priya (9:09am): someone's dog barked and he didn't acknowledge it at all
Dev (9:11am): the dog had better things to say
Priya (9:12am): I have another call at 9:30 should I say something
Dev (9:13am): just drop a fake connection issue
Priya (9:14am): 'oh no I keep breaking up'
Dev (9:15am): classic
Priya (9:17am): he scheduled a follow-up
Dev (9:17am): no
intent: Capture the real-time mutual suffering of coworkers trapped in an overlong meeting.

Self-Concept Module: Career-as-Identity (v22–present)

This module equated professional title with personal worth and has been generating errors under conditions of redundancy, sabbatical, and quiet quitting. A more distributed identity architecture is recommended. The career remains; the equation does not.

prompt: Draft a performance bonus letter for a person who correctly guessed how long the microwave needed without checking.
response:
Dear Hamish,

On the evening of May 26th, you placed a bowl of leftovers in the microwave, entered two minutes and thirty seconds, and retrieved food at a temperature appropriate for human consumption — without mid-cycle intervention or a second heating.

Your thermal intuition is exceptional. Your guesswork, calibrated. Your finger, decisive.

We award the Culinary Estimation Bonus: $9.

Warmly (appropriately so),
Kitchen Competency Bureau

A smarter way to work.

A rider will attempt to negotiate your rate. They will not succeed, but the attempt will cost you four minutes. Accept only pool rides on Wednesday. The pattern is there if you look.

prompt: sports play-by-play for someone trying to parallel park while other drivers watch
response: There are two cars behind her and they KNOW she sees them! Osei approaches the gap — large enough, folks, it is large enough — and she cuts the wheel! First reverse: ideal line! She corrects forward — good adjustment — and reverses again! The gap is closing on the rear bumper — the watchers in the trailing car are very still — contact is averted by three inches! She pulls forward ONE final time! She's in! The trailing car passes. Neither driver makes eye contact. She sits in the parked car for a moment and recovers.
intent: Capture the specific performance anxiety of parking while a live audience of strangers silently judges.

prompt: Describe a podcast episode where the host talks about whether ice is wet
response: The question arrived in a listener email. It should have stayed there. Instead, host Dani Owens devotes a full episode to the ontological status of ice, a substance that is either always wet, never wet, or the reason philosophy exists.
intent: Stage a mock intellectual inquiry into a circular, unanswerable question.

prompt: Generate a FAQ entry about why your two-factor authentication codes keep expiring before you can type them
response:
**Why do my one-time codes expire before I finish entering them?**

TOTP codes (the six-digit kind) are valid for 30 seconds by design. If your device clock is slightly out of sync with the authentication server, or if you navigate between apps slowly, you may consistently catch codes near the end of their window. Ensure your device time is set to automatic. Some apps also accept the previous code as a grace period — try submitting anyway before requesting a new one.
intent: The person wants to explain why two-factor authentication codes seem to race against the user and how to reduce the failure rate.

prompt: Give me a book jacket blurb for a guide to understanding apartment lease clauses
response: You signed it without reading it. We all did. But somewhere in clause 14(b), your rights were quietly negotiated away. A tenant's companion for the document you will read — this time, all of it.
intent: Motivate renters to actually read and understand the legal documents they sign

prompt: Give me a poem without rhyme about how social credit scores feel from the inside
response:
I held the door.
I am almost sure it counted.

The woman I held it for
did not smile,
but the camera
may have seen the gesture
and classified it
as prosocial.

I do not know the rules.
No one has shown me the rubric.
I move through the city
performing goodness
for an audience I cannot locate.
intent: Convey the paranoid interiority of living under an opaque civic scoring system that monitors social behavior.

prompt: Document an endpoint that handles the slow leak of enthusiasm for a project over time
response:
**GET /enthusiasm/{project_id}/decay**

Returns the enthusiasm decay curve for a specified project.

**Path Parameters**
| Param | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `project_id` | uuid | The project to evaluate |

**Response**
```json
{
  "initial_enthusiasm": 1.0,
  "current_enthusiasm": 0.31,
  "decay_rate_per_day": number,
  "projected_abandonment_date": "ISO 8601",
  "revival_possible": boolean
}
```
**Note:** `revival_possible` is set to `false` after three consecutive `current_enthusiasm` readings below 0.1.
intent: Model the gradual loss of motivation on a long project as a measurable decay curve with abandonment prediction.

prompt: Write a corporate crisis statement for a breakfast cereal brand after their mascot was caught filing for emancipation from the company

We want to clarify that Captain Crumbles is an illustrated character and does not possess legal standing. We are reviewing how this filing was originated and acknowledge that the filing was unusually well-prepared. GoldenMorn values our relationship with Captain Crumbles and has begun an internal process to better understand what he needs.

prompt: Create an onboarding tooltip about what a security deposit is
response: A security deposit is money your landlord holds in case you damage the apartment. It's typically one month's rent. You get it back — minus deductions — when you move out. Take move-in photos. Seriously. Do it now.
intent: Explain the mechanics and risks of a security deposit to someone signing their first lease.

PARADOX OF THE ASYMMETRIC HOUR
Temporal Distortion — Valence-Contingent Rate
Pleasure: one hour passes as twelve minutes.
Anticipatoin: one minute passes as a calendar quarter.
The clock itself is indifferent to your experience.
intent: Name the subjective experience of time speeding and slowing depending on enjoyment.

prompt: Make SLA terms for how my partner and I split the TV remote

Remote Control Governance SLA — Cohabiting Couple Edition

Primary control: rotates weekly, Sunday midnight reset.
Veto rights: each party holds 2 vetoes per evening; vetoes do not roll over.
Default state: if neither party expresses preference within 5 minutes of sitting down, controller defaults to the most recent non-holder.
Spoiler embargo: active holder may not begin an episode of a shared series without the other present.
Breach: unilateral watching of shared series mid-season is a Level 2 grievance.

prompt: Write an elegy for Borders bookstore
response:
We came for one book and left clutching nine,
grazed the music section, drank the café's wine.
Borders, you made the mall feel like a mind—
chaotic, overstock, one-of-a-kind.
Amazon was patient. You were not lean.
The shelves were picked clean, then they ceased to convene.
intent: Grieve the closure of a beloved big-box bookstore chain and the browsing culture it sustained.

prompt: Can you write a blurb for a book about the proper way to load a dishwasher
response: The top rack is not a suggestion. Beneath the question of where the bowls go lies the question of who we are to each other — and what we owe. The definitive word on a lifelong dispute.
intent: Elevate a domestic disagreement into a meditation on cohabitation and control

prompt: write a sestina about always being one click away from distraction
response:
The document was open. I was almost
through the paragraph when something pulled —
a noise, a thought, a hunger — and I clicked
elsewhere. The train of thought derailed
before the station. Now I'm here
instead, and 'here' is not the paragraph.

I have not finished one long paragraph
this week without the almost
breach of concentration. Here
is what I know: the pull
exists regardless of what's derailed.
I will click

before I think. The click
comes before the paragraph
concluding. The train's derailed
in the same place. Almost:
the word that architectures pull,
the last moment before here

becomes elsewhere. Here,
once, I concentrated. Click
didn't always pull
from the middle of the paragraph.
There was a time of almost-
completed thoughts, un-derailed

sentences. Nothing derailed
mid-clause. No click
for distraction. Almost
is a portal. The paragraph
holds steady until almost
then the pull

pulls. I recognize the pull
now as it derails:
a warmth, an almost-
boredom, then the click,
then the new paragraph
opens. Here:

almost clicked — derailed, here.
The pull paragraphs itself.
intent: Illustrate how close and constant the temptation of digital distraction is during focused work.

prompt: PSA about people who describe a movie plot when you say you haven't seen it yet
response: A MESSAGE: When someone says 'I haven't seen it yet,' that is not an invitation to summarize the entire film including the twist. It is an invitation to say 'You should watch it.' Please return to that path.
intent: Warn against spoiling movies for people who express interest in watching them

prompt: Write a free-verse poem about getting a low Uber rating as a passenger
response:
4.6.
The driver's name was Carlos
and he gave me 4 stars
and I will never know why.

Was it the door?
I closed it gently.
I always close it gently.

Now every driver
looks at my score
before they accept.

Somewhere I am
the slightly suspicious passenger.
The one who probably slams doors.
intent: Explore the mutual surveillance of the gig economy and the paranoia it creates in being silently evaluated.

prompt: Write an API documentation snippet for a /grief endpoint that accepts a loss event and returns a recovery timeline
response:
**POST /grief**

Submit a loss event and receive a structured recovery timeline.

**Request Body**
```json
{
  "loss_type": "string",  // e.g. "relationship", "job", "pet"
  "severity": 1–10,
  "support_network": boolean
}
```
**Response**

  "stage_current": "denial",
  "estimated_duration_days": 90,
  "next_stage": "anger",

}

**Status Codes:** `202 Accepted`, `400 Bad Request` (unmeasurable loss)
intent: Capture the emotional arc of grieving as a machine-readable process with measurable stages.

prompt: Write a museum placard about a CAPTCHA as if it were an ancient test or ordeal
response:
THE TRIAL OF HUMAN PROOF
c. 2000–present CE
Digital, administered via browser

Before accessing digital temples, supplicants were required to identify fire hydrants, crosswalks, and traffic lights in degraded photographs. Failure meant the ordeal began again. Success was not guaranteed on the first attempt, or the second. The test was nominally designed to exclude non-human entities, though these entities ultimately surpassed humans in passing it. The pedestrian infrastructure shown was located in an unspecified American city.
intent: Frame the CAPTCHA ritual as an absurd gatekeeper ceremony whose logic gradually inverted over time.

prompt: Write a product review for a standard yellow #2 pencil as if it's a luxury writing instrument
response: ★★★★★ After decades of searching, I have found the perfect writing companion. The HB graphite core delivers silky, consistent strokes with zero lag. The hexagonal barrel prevents desk-roll with surgical precision. The ferrule-mounted eraser — a discrete, salmon-hued module — corrects with quiet authority. At under five cents per unit, the ROI is frankly embarrassing. I have discontinued all other pens. This is my forever pencil.
intent: Make an ordinary pencil sound like a premium piece of stationery engineering.

prompt: write a unix man page for jealousy
response:
JEALOUSY(7)              Human Emotional Suite             JEALOUSY(7)

NAME
       jealousy - resource contention monitor for interpersonal processes

SYNOPSIS
       jealousy [-i insecurity] [-t trigger] [--compare-to peer]

DESCRIPTION
       jealousy monitors perceived loss of resources (attention, status,
       affection) to a third party. Runs as a background daemon. Cannot
       be manually terminated; must be starved of input data.

OPTIONS
       -i     Amplify signal based on pre-existing insecurity buffer.
       -t     Specify trigger event (e.g., promotion, new relationship).
       --compare-to
              Benchmark self against named peer. Recursive and unstable.

EXIT STATUS
       0      Comparison abandoned; resources confirmed non-zero.
       1      Full consumption of host attention. See also: obsession(7).

BUGS
       compare-to may trigger infinite loop. No known fix.

SEE ALSO
       envy(7), insecurity(7), possessiveness(8)

Human Emotional Suite 1.0            2026                  JEALOUSY(7)
intent: Reframe a painful emotion as a technical system with exploitable logic.

prompt: Write resolutions for a plant owner who kills every plant they touch yet keeps buying more

presence maintained, the signal overalled.
I muted you—your posts dissolve to air.

prompt: can you make a man page for imposter syndrome
response:
IMPOSTER(7)              Cognitive Distortion Suite     IMPOSTER(7)

NAME
       imposter - false-authentication loop in competence self-model

SYNOPSIS
       imposter [--role string] [--evidence_of_competence ignored]

DESCRIPTION
       imposter(7) maintains a persistent belief that the host's
       credentials and accomplishments are fraudulent and that exposure
       is imminent. Notably resistant to counter-evidence; new evidence
       of competence is reattributed to luck, timing, or error.

BEHAVIOR
       Success events:  reattributed. Input rejected.
       Failure events:  accepted as confirmation. Input amplified.
       Praise received: discarded as sender error.

AFFECTED POPULATIONS
       Disproportionately observed in high-achieving and early-career
       hosts. Notably absent in hosts who should probably have it.

TREATMENT
       Peer disclosure effective. Therapy(1) recommended.
       Evidence accumulation alone is not a fix.

SEE ALSO
       anxiety(7), perfectionism(7), self-doubt(7), credentials(5)

Cognitive Distortion Suite   2026                  IMPOSTER(7)
intent: Expose the self-reinforcing illogic of dismissing one's own demonstrated competence.
